elements.sent-2411-001: Although randomization has long been used in concurrent programs, formal methods for reasoning about this mixture of effects have lagged behind.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, no existing program logics can express specifications about the distributions of outcomes resulting from programs that are both probabilistic and concurrent.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this, we introduce Probabilistic Concurrent Outcome Logic, which incorporates ideas from concurrent and probabilistic separation logics into Outcome Logic to introduce new compositional reasoning principles.
elements.sent-2411-004: Randomization is an important tool in concurrent and distributed computing.
elements.sent-2411-005: For example, concurrent algorithms can be made more efficient using randomization \cite{rabin1980n-process,rabin1982choice,morris1978counting and some synchronization problems---such as Dijkstra's Dining Philosophers---have no deterministic solution \cite{lehmann1981advantages .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: But despite the ubiquity of randomization in concurrent computing, formal methods for these types of programs are limited.
elements.sent-2411-007: The mixture of computational effects displayed in probabilistic concurrent programs is a major source of difficulty in developing such techniques; random choice is introduced by sampling operations and nondeterminism arises from scheduling the concurrent threads.
elements.sent-2411-008: These two types of choice do not compose in standard ways \cite{varacca_winskel_2006 , so even just describing the semantics of these programs requires specialized models \cite{jifeng1997probabilistic,mciver2005abstraction,zilberstein2025demonic .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In this paper, we introduce Probabilistic Concurrent Outcome Logic (\pcol), a logic for reasoning about programs that are both probabilistic and concurrent.
elements.sent-2411-010: In \pcol, preconditions and postconditions are not just assertions about a single program state.
elements.sent-2411-011: Instead, they describe the distribution of possible outcomes that can arise from executing the program.
elements.sent-2411-012: A key challenge is that, in the concurrent setting, different orderings of threads can give rise to different distributions over program behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-013: To address this, \pcol takes inspiration from the recently introduced Demonic Outcome Logic (\dol) \cite{zilberstein2025demonic , which supports reasoning about sequential probabilistic programs that additionally have a nondeterministic choice operator resolved by an adversary.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Different nondeterministic choices can cause different distributions of behaviors in these programs, just as different thread interleavings can cause different distributions in the concurrent setting.
elements.sent-2411-015: While \dol's approach for describing this space of possible distributions provides a basis for \pcol, reasoning about the nondeterminism that arises from concurrent scheduling is substantially more complicated than reasoning about a choice operator.
elements.sent-2411-016: The reason is that the nondeterminism in the latter is localized to the points where the choice operator is used, whereas in a concurrent program, every step can potentially involve nondeterminism from thread interleaving.
elements.sent-2411-017: Reasoning explicitly about this nondeterminism at every step is intractable and non-compositional.
elements.sent-2411-018: To recover compositional reasoning, \pcol{ incorporates ideas from various separation logics.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Concurrent Separation Logic (\csl) uses disjointness of resources to ensure that concurrent computations only interact in controlled ways, so that each thread can be analyzed on its own \cite{csl,brookes2004semantics .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Probabilistic Separation Logic (\psl) uses the notions of independence and conditioning to reason about the interaction between randomness and control flow \cite{psl,li2023lilac,bao2024bluebell .}
elements.sent-2411-021: These logics achieve compositional reasoning in concurrent and probabilistic settings, respectively, so combining their reasoning principles together appears to be a natural way to derive a compositional logic for the combination of these effects.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, as we will see in \Cref{sec:overview , such a combination is challenging to achieve because the semantic models of each logic are quite different and highly specialized to their respective domains.}
elements.sent-2411-023: To combine these reasoning principles in \pcol, we develop a new semantic model that captures causality between actions by threads, while also properly sequencing decisions of the scheduler with probabilistic choices made during the program execution.
elements.sent-2411-025: In \Cref{sec:semantics , we formulate a denotational model of probabilistic concurrency relative to an invariant---a contract about shared state that must remain true at all times.}
elements.sent-2411-026: By contrast, probabilistic assertions require us to model the entire distribution of outcomes, which is more readily done using denotational methods.
elements.sent-2411-027: While denotational models of \csl do exist \cite{brookes2004semantics , the inclusion of probabilistic choice introduces further complexity; interference between threads results in a distribution over shared state, not a single value, and the alteration of shared state must be properly sequenced with random sampling and scheduling of threads.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We develop an invariant sensitive semantics in \Cref{sec:semantics , which ensures that the invariant is always true while retaining the denotation of the program as a distribution of outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-029: We introduce \pcol\ in \Cref{sec:model,sec:logic , the first logic to use probabilistic independence and conditioning to reason about concurrent programs.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Although our logic has familiar inference rules, the soundness proofs for those rules are substantially more complex.
elements.sent-2411-031: For example, in the parallel composition rule (\ruleref{Par ), we not only show that the resources owned by the two threads end up having the proper values, but also that the joint distribution over those values is correct.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This relies on the fact that the nondeterminism introduced by the scheduler is not measurable, a property which has not arisen in prior probabilistic separation logics, since they did not support nondeterminism.
elements.sent-2411-033: In \Cref{sec:examples we give three examples using \pcol to prove correctness of protocols that mix randomness from concurrent threads, and privately retrieve information from a database.}
elements.section-2411-002: Overview: Familiar Reasoning Principles in a New Setting
elements.sent-2411-034: A major hurdle in concurrency analysis is that the semantics of programs is not compositional; two programs can have completely different behavior when run in parallel than they do when run in isolation.
elements.sent-2411-035: Reasoning about programs by enumerating all possible interleavings of the threads is not a viable strategy, so restrictions must be introduced to enable sound compositional analysis.
elements.sent-2411-036: This can be achieved via separation---if two concurrent threads act on disjoint regions of memory, then their behavior will not change when run in parallel---giving rise to the \hyperlink{rule:par1 {Par rule of Concurrent Separation Logic \cite{csl,brookes2004semantics , where the separating conjunction $\varphi\sep\psi$ means that the machines's resources (\ie memory cells) can be divided to satisfy $\varphi$ and $\psi$ individually. \begin{equation}\hypertarget{rule:par1}{ \inferrule{ \triple{\varphi_1}{C_1}{\psi_1} \triple{\varphi_2}{C_2}{\psi_2} }{ \triple{\varphi_1\sep\varphi_2}{C_1\parallel C_2}{\psi_1\sep \psi_2} }{Par} }\end{equation} We now ask, what if $C_1$ and $C_2$ are probabilistic programs? For example, suppose that we wanted to flip two coins in parallel and store the results in the variables $x$ and $y$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: After running each thread, the respective variables will be distributed according to Bernoulli distributions with parameter $\frac12$, as shown in the following specifications, where $\sure P$ means that $P$ holds with probability 1 (almost surely), ${x\mapsto -}$ means that the current thread has permission to read and write $x$, and $x \sim d$ means that $x$ is distributed according to $d$. \begin{equation}\triple{\sure{x\mapsto -}}{x \samp \bern{\tfrac12}}{x \sim \bern{\tfrac12}} \qquad\quad \triple{\sure{y\mapsto -}}{y \samp \bern{\tfrac12}}{y \sim \bern{\tfrac12}}\end{equation} After composing these two programs in parallel, the final specification will ideally dictate not only how $x$ and $y$ are distributed, but also their joint distribution.
elements.sent-2411-038: This is achieved using a probabilistic interpretation of the separating conjunction $\varphi\sep\psi$, stating that the events described by $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are probabilistically independent \cite{psl .}
elements.sent-2411-039: If the \hyperlink{rule:par1 {Par rule held under this probabilistic interpretation, we could derive the following specification, which is indeed valid in this case. \begin{equation}\triple{\sure{x\mapsto -} \sep \sure{y\mapsto -}}{x \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \parallel y \samp \bern{\tfrac12}}{x \sim \bern{\tfrac12} \sep y \sim \bern{\tfrac12}}\end{equation} Here, the independence guarantee of the separating conjunction means that each outcome (\eg $x$ and $y$ are both 1) occurs with probability $\frac12\times\frac12=\frac14$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Intuitively, a probabilistic interpretation of \hyperlink{rule:par1 {Par is justifiable because $C_1$ and $C_2$ execute without interaction, so the there is no correlation between the random behaviors that they introduce.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Bigger challenges arise when there is shared state, which is handled with invariants in \csl.
elements.sent-2411-042: An invariant $I$ is an assertion about shared state that must remain true at all times.
elements.sent-2411-043: Threads can interact with shared state as long as $I$ is preserved by every action.
elements.sent-2411-044: This is achieved in \csl with the following inference rules; a more general \hyperlink{rule:par2 {Par rule separates the pre- and postcondition, but allows the invariant to be used in both threads.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The \hyperlink{rule:atom2 {Atom rule opens the invariant, as long as the program is a single atomic commands $a$. \begin{equation}\hypertarget{rule:par2}{ \inferrule{ I\vdash\triple{\varphi_1}{C_1}{\psi_1} I\vdash\triple{\varphi_2}{C_2}{\psi_2} }{ I\vdash\triple{\varphi_1\sep\varphi_2}{C_1\parallel C_2}{\psi_1\sep \psi_2} }{Par} } \qquad\qquad \hypertarget{rule:atom2}{ \inferrule{ \vdash\triple{\varphi\sep\sure I}a{\psi\sep \sure I} }{ I\vdash\triple\varphi{a}\psi }{Atom} }\end{equation} Under a probabilistic interpreation of $\sep$, this stronger \hyperlink{rule:par2 {Par rule is valid when the shared state described by the invariant $I$ is deterministic, for example, as in: \begin{equation}z \coloneq 1 \fatsemi \left( x\samp \bern{\tfrac{z}2} \parallel y \samp \bern{\tfrac{z}2} \right)\end{equation} However, when the shared state is randomized, then the rule is unsound: if the threads interact with shared state, then the final events may be correlated, such as in the following program where both $x$ and $y$ are derived from $z$. \begin{equation}\label{eq:conditioning} z \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \fatsemi ( x\coloneqq z \parallel y \coloneqq 1-z )\end{equation} Here, $x$ and $y$ are not independently distributed, so how can we enable compositional reasoning when shared state is randomized? The key observation is that in example (\ref{eq:conditioning ), although $x$ and $y$ are clearly not independently distributed, their distributions are conditionally independent on $z$.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\hypertarget{rule:par2}{ \inferrule{ I\vdash\triple{\varphi_1}{C_1}{\psi_1} I\vdash\triple{\varphi_2}{C_2}{\psi_2} }{ I\vdash\triple{\varphi_1\sep\varphi_2}{C_1\parallel C_2}{\psi_1\sep \psi_2} }{Par} } \qquad\qquad \hypertarget{rule:atom2}{ \inferrule{ \vdash\triple{\varphi\sep\sure I}a{\psi\sep \sure I} }{ I\vdash\triple\varphi{a}\psi }{Atom} }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: Therefore, we can first condition on the possible outcomes of the shared state, thereby determinizing it, and then use the \hyperlink{rule:par2 {Par rule.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Logically, conditioning corresponds to breaking down the outcomes of the sampling operation into cases, a common operation in both Outcome Logic \cite{outcome,zilberstein2024relatively,zilberstein2025demonic and the probabilistic separation logics Lilac~\cite{li2023lilac and Bluebell~\cite{bao2024bluebell .}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We expand the $z \sim \bern{\frac12}$ assertion using an outcome conjunction ${\bigoplus_{Z\sim\bern{\frac12 \sure{z\mapsto Z }$, which binds a new logical variable $Z$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: We can then reason about each outcome individually, where $z$ is deterministic, so the \hyperlink{rule:par2 {Par applies to obtain the following spec (the full derivation is shown in \Aref{app:examples , \Aref{fig:overview-full-deriv ). \begin{equation}z\mapsto Z \vdash \triple{\sure{x\mapsto -}\sep\sure{y\mapsto-}}{x \coloneqq z \parallel y \coloneqq 1-z }{\sure{x \mapsto Z} \sep \sure{y\mapsto 1-Z}}\end{equation} While the outcome conjunction above resembles the conditioning modalities from Lilac and Bluebell, its semantics is tailored for a concurrency logic.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: As we explain in \Cref{sec:prob-assert , the conditioning rules used in those logics do not accommodate resources owned by parallel threads.}
elements.sent-2411-051: As a result, our outcome conjunction has a different semantics, based on the measure theoretic notion of direct sums \cite{fremlin2001measure , which makes our conditioning rules (\ruleref{Cond1 and \ruleref{Cond2 ) fully compositional.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: While it may seem like a natural idea to reinterpret \csl's \hyperlink{rule:par2 {Par rule using probabilistic separation, the semantic model needed to support this sort of reasoning is considerably more complex.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Our semantics needs to capture both the operational notion that the distribution over shared state may change between each step of computation, while also retaining the denotation of the program as a distribution of outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-054: In addition, the nondeterminism introduced by scheduling concurrent threads makes independence very subtle, even without shared state.
elements.sent-2411-055: For example, in the following program, the scheduler can choose to make $y$ always equal to $x$ at the end of the program execution, thereby introducing a correlation even though $x$ and $y$ are not even used in the same thread. \begin{equation}x \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \parallel y \coloneqq 0 \parallel y\coloneqq 1\end{equation} This is achieved by executing the sampling operation first, and then, depending on the outcome, choosing the order of assignments to $y$ so that $y$ is first assigned $1-x$ and then is overridden to $x$.
elements.sent-2411-056: To combat this, we bring the notion of precise assertions from standard separation logic \cite{semanticsep into the probabilistic setting to ensure that the effects of nondeterminism are not measurable.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Requiring every event to have a precise probability means that all we can say about $y$ in the program above is that $\sure{y \in \{0,1\}}$, an event with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-058: This allows us to establish the soundness of \ruleref{Par ; the crux of the proof is to show that if two events have precise probabilities $p$ and $q$ when executed individually, then they both occur with probability $p\cdotp q$ (\Aref{lem:obs-ind1 ) when executed in parallel.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The proof is considerably more complex than that of standard \csl, since it must track probabilities of events in addition to physical resources.
elements.sent-2411-060: We begin the technical development in \Cref{sec:semantics by defining a denotational model for probabilistic concurrent programs.}
elements.sent-2411-061: We then give a measure theoretic model of assertions---including the separation and outcome conjunctions--- in \Cref{sec:model .}
elements.sent-2411-062: In \Cref{sec:logic , we define the validity of \pcol triples and provide a proof system.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Three examples are shown in \Cref{sec:examples before we conclude by discussing related work in \Cref{sec:related and limitations and future work in \Cref{sec:limitations .}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: We begin by describing the syntax and semantics of the programming language that we will be using throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-066: Programs are represented as commands $C\in\mathsf{Cmd}$, which consist of no-ops ($\skp$), sequential composition ($C_1\fatsemi C_2$), parallel composition ($C_1\parallel C_2$), if statements, bounded loops, and actions $a\in\act$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Whereas commands consist of standard operations for a concurrent language, the novelty lies in actions, which can perform probabilistic sampling operations $x \samp d(e)$, where $d\in\mathsf{Dist}$ is a discrete probability distribution with the expression $e$ as a parameter.
elements.sent-2411-068: We include three types of distributions---Bernoulli distributions $\bern p$, assigning probability $p$ to $1$ and probability $1-p$ to $0$; geometric distributions $\geo p$, assigning probability $(1-p)^n p$ to each $n \in\mathbb N$; and uniform distributions $\unif e$ where $e$ evaluates to a finite list of values $[v_1, \ldots, v_n]$, that are then each returned with probability $1/n$.
elements.sent-2411-069: In addition to sampling, we also have deterministic assignment $x\coloneqq e$, where $e$ is an expression.
elements.sent-2411-070: Expressions consist of variables $x$, values $v$, tests $b$, list literals $[e_1, \ldots, e_n]$, list accesses $e[e']$ (where $e$ is a list and $e'$ is an index), and standard arithmetic operations.
elements.sent-2411-071: Many more actions could be added to this semantics, including nondeterministic assignment and atomic concurrency primitives such as compare-and-swap, but we do not explore them in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-072: The addition of random sampling presents several challenges in defining a proper semantics for the language, as no existing techniques are adequate.
elements.sent-2411-073: Concurrent semantics for purely nondeterministic languages are often given operationally by a small step relation.
elements.sent-2411-074: This approach is not ideal for probabilistic computation, since we want to know not only the states that the program could step to, but also the distribution over those states.
elements.sent-2411-075: In denotational semantics, concurrency is often dealt with using partially ordered multisets (pomsets), which record the causality between actions in the program \cite{gischer1988equational,pratt1986modeling .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Each pomset represents the possible interleavings of actions within a single trace, but if the program has multiple traces due to control flow branching such as if statements, then a single pomset is insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-077: Instead, a pomset language---a set of pomsets---is used, where each pomset in the language corresponds to a particular sequence of branches that the program execution may take.
elements.sent-2411-078: The problem with pomset languages is that they lose information about when the decision takes place.
elements.sent-2411-079: This effectively forces the scheduler to decide whether or not a test will be true before that test is resolved, and presents problems when the language contains randomization, since the test may depend on probabilistic sampling operations that occur later in the execution.
elements.sent-2411-080: We solve this problem with a new pomset structure, which we describe in \Cref{sec:pomset .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Next, in \Cref{sec:convex , we describe the interpretation of actions that are both probabilistic and nondeterministic using the convex powerset monad \cite{morgan1996refinement,jacobs2008coalgebraic,tix2000convex,mislove2000nondeterminism .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Finally, in \Cref{sec:linearization , we explain how to convert the pomset semantics into a state transformer, so that we can reason about the behavior of programs.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: Trace Semantics with Uninterpreted Actions and Tests
elements.sent-2411-083: We begin by giving a trace semantics for commands, leaving actions and tests uninterpreted for the time being.
elements.sent-2411-084: The purpose of using uninterpreted atoms is to keep the semantics compositional; since the evaluation of those atoms may depend on the way that they are interleaved, we cannot evaluate them unless we know that no other threads will be composed in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-085: As we mentioned previously, interpreting a probabilistic and concurrent language requires a new semantic structure, as pomset languages do not account for the proper sequencing of probabilistic and nondeterministic actions.
elements.sent-2411-086: This new structure is able to record guarded branching behavior in addition to causality; we call it a pomset with tests.
elements.sent-2411-087: A pomset with tests, similarly to the classical case, will be an isomorphism class of tuples.
elements.sent-2411-088: In our case, we will need 4-tuples $\tuple{L, \le, T, \lambda}$, which we will now describe.
elements.sent-2411-089: The first component $L \subseteq_{\mathsf{fin \mathsf{Label}$ is a finite set of labels, representing the nodes in the pomset.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The relation $\mathord{\le \subseteq L\times L$ is a partial order on $L$, which represents causality; if $\ell \le \ell'$, then $\ell$ must be scheduled before $\ell'$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Our new addition (to classical pomsets) is the function $T \colon \mathbb{B \to L\times L$, which we call the test relation.}
elements.sent-2411-092: If $(\ell, \ell') \in T(1)$, then $\ell'$ can only be scheduled if the test represented by node $\ell$ passes, and similarly, $(\ell, \ell') \in T(0)$ means that $\ell'$ can only be scheduled if the test at node $\ell$ fails.
elements.sent-2411-093: We additionally require that $T(b) \subseteq \mathord{\le}$ for all $b\in\mathbb B$, to ensure that tests are evaluated before the actions that depend on them.
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, $\lambda \colon L \to \act +\test$ is a labelling function, which assigns an action or a test to each node $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-095: We say that two such structures are isomorphic if they are equal modulo relabelling.
elements.sent-2411-096: More precisely, $\tuple{L,\le,T,\lambda \equiv \tuple{L',\le',T',\lambda'}$ iff there exists a bijection $f \colon L\to L'$ such that (1) $\ell \le \ell'$ iff $f(\ell) \le' f(\ell')$; (2) $(\ell, \ell) \in T(b)$ iff $(f(\ell), f(\ell')) \in T'(b)$; and (3) $\lambda(\ell) = \lambda'(f(\ell))$ for all $\ell, \ell' \in L$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: An isomorphism class of these 4-tuples can then be defined in the usual way: \begin{equation}\isotuple{L, \le, T, \lambda} = \{ \tuple{L', \le', T', \lambda'} \mid \tuple{L, \le, T, \lambda}\equiv\tuple{L', \le', T', \lambda'} \}\end{equation} A {\em pomset with tests is an isomorphism class on these structures, that is an element of the set: \begin{equation}\pom \triangleq \{ \isotuple{L, \le, T, \lambda} \mid \tuple{L, \le, T, \lambda} ~ as described above \}\end{equation} Since we only use pomsets with tests in this paper, from now on we refer to them as simply pomsets.}
elements.sent-2411-098: When we define operations on pomsets, we will define them on a single representative structure, however the fact that they are an isomorphism class will allow us to assume that any two pomsets have disjoint sets of labels (if they were not disjoint, then we could simply pick an isomorphic representative structure that is disjoint).
elements.sent-2411-099: Going forward, we will use the metavariables $\alpha,\beta$ to denote pomsets, and for some pomset $\alpha$, we let $L_\alpha$, $\le_\alpha$, $T_\alpha$, and $\lambda_\alpha$ be the constituent parts of its internal structure.
elements.sent-2411-100: We can now define several operations on pomsets.
elements.sent-2411-101: First of all, the empty pomset $\emptypom \triangleq \{ \tuple{\emptyset, \emptyset, T_\emptyset, \lambda_\emptyset \}$ has an empty label set, an empty order, and $T\empty(b) = \emptyset$ and $\lambda_\emptyset\colon\emptyset\to \act+\test$ is the trivial function that takes no inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-102: The following function constructs singleton pomsets: \begin{equation}singleton(x) \triangleq \{ \tuple{\{\ell\}, \{\ell,\ell\}, T_\emptyset, \lambda_{\ell,x}} \mid \ell \in Label \} \qquadwhere\quad \lambda_{\ell, x}(\ell) = x\end{equation} We also provide two injection functions for constructing coproducts from actions and tests: \begin{equation}\inj_\act \colon \act \to \act+\test \qquad\qquad \inj_\test \colon \test \to \act+\test\end{equation} We define the minimal elements of a pomset to be all the labels $\ell$ for which no other label is smaller than $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-103: Operationally speaking, those minimal nodes are the ones that are ready to be scheduled. \begin{equation}\min(\alpha) \triangleq \{ \ell \in L_\alpha \mid \forall \ell' \in \ell.\ \ell' \le_\alpha \ell \Rightarrow \ell' = \ell \}\end{equation} We now define an operator $\fatsemi$, which performs sequential composition by joining the label sets and making every element of the first set smaller than every element of the second.
elements.sent-2411-104: We achieve this using a sequential composition operation on partial orders $\mathord{\fatsemi \colon L\times L \to L'\times L' \to (L\uplus L') \times (L\uplus L')$. \begin{equation}\mathord{\le}\fatsemi\mathord{\le'} \triangleq \mathord{\le}\cup\mathord{\le'}\cup (L \times L')\end{equation} We lift this to a $\fatsemi$ operation on pomsets, by using a representative structure of the pomset.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Parallel composition $\parallel$ is similar, but there is no causality between the nodes in the two pomsets, so we take a standard union of the partial orders. \begin{equation}\alpha \fatsemi \beta \triangleq \isotuple{L_\alpha\uplus L_\beta, \mathord{\le_\alpha}\fatsemi\mathord{\le_\beta}, T_\alpha\uplus T_\beta, \lambda_\alpha\uplus\lambda_\beta} \qquad \alpha \parallel \beta \triangleq \isotuple{L_\alpha\uplus L_\beta, \mathord{\le_\alpha}\cup\mathord{\le_\beta}, T_\alpha\uplus T_\beta, \lambda_\alpha\uplus\lambda_\beta}\end{equation} Whereas all of the definitions above have been relatively standard, we now define a novel guarding operation $\mathsf{guard \colon \test \times \pom\times \pom \to\pom$, which is how we construct pomsets that contain information about the control flow structure of programs.}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\alpha \fatsemi \beta \triangleq \isotuple{L_\alpha\uplus L_\beta, \mathord{\le_\alpha}\fatsemi\mathord{\le_\beta}, T_\alpha\uplus T_\beta, \lambda_\alpha\uplus\lambda_\beta} \qquad \alpha \parallel \beta \triangleq \isotuple{L_\alpha\uplus L_\beta, \mathord{\le_\alpha}\cup\mathord{\le_\beta}, T_\alpha\uplus T_\beta, \lambda_\alpha\uplus\lambda_\beta}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: More precisely, $\mathsf{guard (b, \alpha, \beta)$ will ensure that the pomset $\alpha$ is executed if $b$ resolves to true, and $\beta$ will get executed in $b$ resolves to false.}
elements.sent-2411-107: We define this operation as follows: \begin{equation}\guard(b, \alpha, \beta) \triangleq \isotuple{L, \le, T, \lambda}\end{equation} Where the structure $\isotuple{L, \le, T, \lambda}$ is constructed as follows.
elements.sent-2411-108: Let $\tuple {\{\ell\}, \le', T', \lambda' = \mathsf{singleton (\inj_\test(b))$ be some representative structure.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The set of labels $L \triangleq \{\ell\} \uplus L_\alpha\uplus L_\beta$ is the set of labels from both branches, plus the new label for the test.
elements.sent-2411-110: The partial order $\mathord{\le' \triangleq (\{\ell\} \times L) \cup \mathord{\le_\alpha}\cup \mathord{\le_\beta}$ augments the orders of the branches by adding a causal relationship from the test node to everything else.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The test relation is $T(1) \triangleq (\{\ell\} \times L_\alpha) \cup T_\alpha(1) \cup T_\beta(1)$ and $T(0) \triangleq (\{\ell\} \times L_\beta) \cup T_\alpha(0) \cup T_\beta(0)$ adds dependencies from the test node to the two branches, selecting the appropriate branch to correspond to whether the test is true or false.
elements.sent-2411-112: Finally, the labelling function $\lambda$ simply agrees with the labelings of the branches, or returns $\inj_\test(b)$ on the new label.
elements.sent-2411-113: We are now ready to define the semantics of our language, shown in \Cref{fig:trace-semantics .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The operations of the commands correspond exactly to the pomset operations that we just described, with the exception of loops, which simply repeat the command $C$ the appropriate number of times.

elements.sent-2411-117: We now discuss the semantics of actions and tests.
elements.sent-2411-118: Actions must be interpreted in a domain that supports both probabilistic and nondeterministic computation.
elements.sent-2411-119: Although none of our actions are explicitly nondeterministic, nondeterminism arises due to the interleaving of concurrent threads.
elements.sent-2411-120: The difficulty is that typical representations of probabilistic computation (distributions) do not compose well with typical representations of nondeterminism (powersets) \cite{varacca_winskel_2006,zwart2019no .}
elements.sent-2411-121: We instead use the convex powerset $\C$ in the denotational semantics of actions, whose type is shown below: \begin{equation}\de{-}_\act \colon \act \to \mem S \to \C(\mem S) \qquadand\qquad \de{-}_\test \colon \test \to \mem S \to \mathbb B,\end{equation} Above, $\mem S$ is a memory over a set of variables $S$ and $\mathbb B = \{0, 1\}$ is the Booleans.
elements.sent-2411-122: These components will be described throughout the remainder of the section.
elements.sent-2411-123: A memory $\sigma \in \mem S$ is a mapping from a particular set of variables $S \subseteq \mathsf{Var}$ to values, that is, $\mem S \triangleq S\to \mathsf{Val}$, where values $\mathsf{Val}$ consist of integers, rationals, and lists.
elements.sent-2411-124: We can combine two memories using the disjoint union $\mathord{\uplus}\colon \mem S \to \mem T \to \mem{S\cup T}$ as long as $S\cap T=\emptyset$, and we define a similar operation $A\sep B$ on sets of memories $A\subseteq \mem S$ and $B\subseteq \mem T$. \begin{equation}(\sigma\uplus\tau)(x) \triangleq \left\{ {ll} \sigma(x) if~ x\in S \tau(x) if~ x\in T  \right. \qquad A\sep B \triangleq \{ \sigma \uplus \tau \mid \sigma\in A, \tau \in B \}\end{equation} The notation $A\sep B$ is reminiscent of the separating conjunction \cite{bi , and indeed we will use it in \Cref{sec:logic to define the separating conjunction.}}}

elements.sent-2411-125: Dually, we define projections $\pi_S \colon \mem T \to \mem{S\cap T}$ as $\pi_S(\sigma)(x) \triangleq \sigma(x)$ if $x\in S$.
elements.sent-2411-126: Expressions are interpreted in the usual way with $\de{e _\expr \colon \mem S \to \mathsf{Val}$ as long as $\mathsf{fv (e) \subseteq S$, if not then $\de{e _\expr(\sigma)$ is undefined.}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: The same is true for tests and $\de{b _\test\colon \mem S\to \mathbb B$ is defined as long as $\mathsf{fv (b) \subseteq S$.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: A discrete probability distribution $\mu \in \D(X)$ over a countable set $X$ is a mapping from elements of $X$ to $[0,1]$ such that $\sum_{x\in X}\mu(x) = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-129: The support of a distribution is the set of elements to which it assigns nonzero probability $\supp(\mu) \triangleq \{ x \in X \mid \mu(x) \neq 0 \}$.
elements.sent-2411-130: The Dirac, or point-mass, distribution $\delta_x$ assigns probability 1 to $x$ and $0$ to everything else.
elements.sent-2411-131: We also extend the previously defined projections to distributions $\pi_S \colon \D(\mem T) \to \D(\mem{S\cap T )$ by marginalizing as follows $\pi_S(\mu)(\sigma) \triangleq {\sum_{\tau \in \mem{T \setminus S \mu(\sigma\uplus\tau)$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-133: We call a set of distributions $S\subseteq \D(X)$ convex if it is closed under convex combinations.
elements.sent-2411-134: That is, $(\mu \oplus_p \nu) \in S$ for every $\mu,\nu\in S$ and $p\in[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Our domain of computation consists of nonempty and convex sets of distributions. \begin{equation}\C(X) \triangleq \{ S \subseteq \D(X + \{\bot\}) \mid S ~is nonempty and convex \}\end{equation} We include $\bot$ to represent undefined behavior such as accessing a variable that is not in the current memory.
elements.sent-2411-136: Non-emptiness ensures that the semantics is not vacuous, since undefined behavior is represented by $\{ \delta_\bot\}$ rather than $\emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-137: Finally, convexity ensures that $\C$ carries a monad structure \cite{jacobs2008coalgebraic , making the sequencing of actions compositional.}
elements.sent-2411-138: More precisely, there is a unit $\eta \colon X \to \C(X)$ and Kleisli extension $(-)^\dagger \colon (X \to \C(Y)) \to \C(X) \to \C(Y)$, which obey the monad laws: $\eta^\dagger = \mathsf{id}$, $f^\dagger \circ \eta = f$, and $f^\dagger \circ g^\dagger = (f^\dagger \circ g)^\dagger$.
elements.sent-2411-139: These operations are defined as follows: \begin{equation}\eta(x) \triangleq \{ \delta_x \} \qquad f^\dagger(S) \triangleq \left\{ \smashoperator[r]{\sum_{x\in\supp(\mu)}} \mu(x)\cdot \nu_x ~ \Big| ~ \mu\in S, \forall x \in \supp(\mu).\ \nu_x \in f_\bot(x) \right\}\end{equation} Where above $f_\bot(x) = f(x)$ for $x\in X$ and $f_\bot(\bot) = \eta(\bot)$.
elements.sent-2411-140: As an overloading of notation, we will occasionally write $f^\dagger(\mu)$ to mean $f^\dagger(\{\mu\})$.

elements.sent-2411-147: In the next section, we will see how `$\nd$' will be used to represent the choices of the scheduler when interleaving concurrent threads.
elements.sent-2411-148: The fact that $S\nd T$ is represented as a set of convex combinations operationally corresponds to the idea that the scheduler can use randomness to choose between $S$ and $T$, rather than making the choice deterministically \cite{varacca2002powerdomain .}
elements.sent-2411-149: Before building a logic to reason about programs, we need a way to interpret programs as state transformers, which we achieve using a linearization operation $\lin \colon \pom \to \mem S \to \C(\mem S)$.
elements.sent-2411-150: Typical linearization operations map pomsets to sets of linearly ordered traces, which can then be interpreted \cite{gischer1988equational , however that approach is insufficient in the presence of probabilistic choice.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Instead, $\lin$ must ensure that scheduling of actions is properly sequenced with respect to probabilistic choices, so that we properly model the scheduler's ability to observe the outcomes of random sampling events, as we discussed in \Cref{sec:overview .}
elements.sent-2411-152: Linearizing makes the semantics no longer compositional, so it is only suitable for determining the semantics of a complete program, or for two programs that can only interact with shared state in specific ways, as we will see in \Cref{sec:logic .}
elements.sent-2411-153: As we alluded to in \Cref{sec:overview , the way in which we will reason about shared state is via invariants---assertions about shared state that must be preserved by every atomic action.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Because the shared state may be modified at any instant by another thread, we define an invariant sensitive semantics, in which the scheduler may alter shared state before executing each atomic action.
elements.sent-2411-155: This will be based on semantic invariants, finite sets of memories $\I \subseteq_\mathsf{fin \mem T$ that represent the legal states, and must include only variables that are disjoint from the standard program states.}
elements.sent-2411-156: We limit invariants to be finite sets in order to avoid issues related to unbounded nondeterminism \cite{apt1986countable .}
elements.sent-2411-157: We start by defining invariant sensitive action evaluation $\de{a _\act^\I \colon \mem S \to \C(\mem S)$.. \begin{equation}\de{a}^{\I}_\act(\sigma) \triangleq \bignd_{\tau\in \I} \left\{ {ll} \pi_S\left(\de{a}_\act(\sigma\uplus\tau)\right) if~ \bigcup \{ \pi_T(\supp(\mu)) \mid {\mu \in \de{a}_\act(\sigma\uplus\tau)} \} \subseteq\I \eta(\bot) otherwise  \right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: The convex union over $\tau \in\I$ represents the scheduler choosing a memory $\tau$ to satisfy the invariant.
elements.sent-2411-159: The action $a$ is then evaluated normally on the combined memory $\sigma\uplus\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-160: If the $T$ projection of every possible resulting state remains within $\I$, then the invariant is upheld and so we get the $S$ projection of the result.
elements.sent-2411-161: If not, then the invariant was violated, so we return $\eta(\bot)$ to indicate that the execution is faulty.
elements.sent-2411-162: Letting $\emp \in \mem\emptyset$ be the empty memory, we remark that $\de{a _\act = \de{a _\act^{\{\emp\}}$, meaning that invariant sensitive execution using the empty invariant is equal to normal execution.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Next, we must define two removal operations on pomsets.
elements.sent-2411-164: First, we let $\alpha\setminus L'$ simply remove the labels in $L'$ from the pomset, and accordingly removes them from the relations and labelling function.
elements.sent-2411-165: In addition, we define a filtering operation as follows: \begin{equation}filter(\alpha, \ell, b, \sigma) \triangleq \alpha \setminus \left(\ell \cup \left\{ \ell' \in L_\alpha \mid (\ell, \ell') \in T_\alpha\left(\lnot\de{b}_\test(\sigma)\right) \right\}\right)\end{equation} This operation removes the nodes from $\alpha$ that disagree on the outcome of the test $b$, evaluated at state $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-166: Note that this is not equivalent to keeping the nodes that do agree with $b$, since many nodes do not depend on $b$ at all.
elements.sent-2411-167: It also removes $\ell$, the label of $b$, since $\ell$ has already been executed.
elements.sent-2411-168: We are now ready to define invariant sensitive linearization.
elements.sent-2411-169: Given a semantic invariant $\I \subseteq_\mathsf{fin \mem T$ such that $S\cap T =\emptyset$, we define $\lin^\I \colon \pom \to \mem S \to \C(\mem S)$ inductively on the structure of the pomset, where below we assume that $\alpha$ is nonempty.. \begin{equation}\lin^\I(\emptypom)(\sigma) \triangleq \eta(\sigma) \qquad \lin^\I(\alpha)(\sigma) \triangleq \smashoperator[l]{\bignd_{\ell\in \min(\alpha)}} \left\{ {ll} \lin^\I(\alpha \setminus \{\ell\})^\dagger(\de{a}_{Act}^\I(\sigma)) if~ \lambda_\alpha(\ell) = \inj_\act(a) \lin^\I(filter(\alpha, \ell, b, \sigma))(\sigma) if~ \lambda_\alpha(\ell) = \inj_\test(b)  \right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-170: An empty pomset contains no actions, so its linearization is just the unit function.
elements.sent-2411-171: For a nonempty pomset, the scheduler first picks some minimal node $\ell$ to schedule.
elements.sent-2411-172: If $\ell$ represents an action, then the action is evaluated in the invariant sensitive way, and then the result is sequenced with the evaluation of the remainder of the pomset using Kleisli composition.
elements.sent-2411-173: If $\ell$ is instead a test, then the pomset is filtered based on the resolution of the test on memory $\sigma$, and then run on $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-174: Linearization has several expected properties, as shown below.
elements.sent-2411-175: That is, it behaves well with respect to sequential composition, if statements, and bounded looping (\Aref{lem:lin-seq,lem:lin-guard,lem:lin-repeat ). \begin{equation}\lin^\I(\de{C_1 \fatsemi C_2})(\sigma) = \lin^\I(\de{C_2})^\dagger(\lin^\I(\de{C_1})(\sigma)) \qquad\quad \lin^\I(\de{\forloop nC})(\sigma) = \lin^\I(\de{C})^n(\sigma)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\lin^\I(\de{\iftf b{C_1}{C_2}})(\sigma) = \left\{ {ll} \lin^\I(\de{C_1})(\sigma) if~ \de{b}_\test(\sigma) = \tru \lin^\I(\de{C_2})(\sigma) if~ \de{b}_\test(\sigma) = \fls  \right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Linearization also obeys a monotonicity property (\Aref{lem:inv-mono-eval ) with respect to invariants.}
elements.sent-2411-178: That is, moving more resources into the invariant can only add more behaviors, as long as $\bot$ does not appear in the result set.

u \in \lin^{\I\sep \J (\alpha)(\sigma)$, then:  egin{equation} orall 	au \in \I.\quad \pi_S\left (\lin^\J(\alpha)(\sigma\uplus	au) ight) \quad\subseteq\quad \lin^{\I\sep \J}(\alpha)(\sigma)\end{equation} Let $\lin 	riangleq \lin^{\{\emp\}}$ and note that in $\lin(\alpha)(\sigma)$, the scheduler does not introduce any new behavior, since all actions are evaluated according to $\de{a _\act^{\{\emp\} = \de{a _\act$, so $\lin(\alpha)(\sigma)$ can be viewed as the true semantics of the program.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: The monotonicity lemma above guarantees that adding an invariant will only add new behaviors, so safety properties about $\lin^\I(\alpha)(\sigma)$ will automatically carry over to $\lin(\alpha)(\sigma\uplus\tau)$ for any $\tau\in\I$, which shows up in the soundness of the \ruleref{Share rule later on.}
elements.sent-2411-181: In this section, we discuss the assertions that we use as pre- and postconditions in Probabilistic Concurrent Outcome Logic (\pcol).
elements.sent-2411-182: These assertions are inspired by standard Outcome Logic \cite{outcome,zilberstein2024relatively , but we reinterpret the outcome conjunction as a conditioning modality like those found in the probabilistic separation logics Lilac \cite{li2023lilac and Bluebell \cite{bao2024bluebell .}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Like Lilac and Bluebell, we use probability spaces rather than distributions as models of our assertions.
elements.sent-2411-184: Unlike distributions, which assign probability to every memory, probability spaces only assign mass to measurable events, which are sets of memories in this case.
elements.sent-2411-185: As such, these logics are able to express that particular events are independent, rather than the entire distributions resulting from the program execution.
elements.sent-2411-186: For example, in the program $x \samp \bern{\frac12 \fatsemi y \coloneqq x$, clearly $x$ and $y$ are correlated, but the assertion $\left(x\sim\bern{\frac12}\right) \sep \sure{y \in \{0,1\}}$ is still valid, since the event $y \in \{0,1\}$ occurs with probability 1, and we cannot measure anything finer.}
elements.sent-2411-187: To formalize these ideas, we begin by giving the syntax and semantics for basic assertions about memories in \Cref{sec:basic-assertions .}
elements.sent-2411-188: Next, we discuss background on measure theory and probability spaces in \Cref{sec:measure .}
elements.sent-2411-189: Finally, we give our definition of probabilistic assertions in \Cref{sec:prob-assert .}
elements.sent-2411-190: We begin by describing pure (non-probabilistic) assertions, which are inspired by standard separation logic \cite{localreasoning,sl , but where memories range over variables rather than heap cells, as we discussed in \Cref{sec:convex .}}
elements.sent-2411-191: The syntax for these assertions are shown below. \begin{equation}P \Coloneqq \tru \mid \fls\mid P\land Q \mid P\lor Q\mid P \sep Q \mid \exists X.\ P \mid e \mapsto E \mid E_1 \asymp E_2 E \Coloneqq X \mid v \mid E_1 + E_2 \mid E_1 \cdot E_2 \mid \cdots\end{equation} In addition to expressions and variables from \Cref{sec:semantics , assertions also depend on logical variables $X,Y,Z\in\mathsf{LVar}$, which cannot be modified by programs.}
elements.formula-2411-028: \begin{equation}P \Coloneqq \tru \mid \fls\mid P\land Q \mid P\lor Q\mid P \sep Q \mid \exists X.\ P \mid e \mapsto E \mid E_1 \asymp E_2 E \Coloneqq X \mid v \mid E_1 + E_2 \mid E_1 \cdot E_2 \mid \cdots\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-192: Logical expressions $E \in \mathsf{LExp}$ mirror standard ones, but operate over logical variables $X\in\mathsf{LVar}$ rather than $x\in\mathsf{Var}$.
elements.sent-2411-193: Logical expression evaluation under a context $\Gamma \colon \mathsf{LExp \to \mathsf{Val}$ is written $\de{E _{\mathsf{LExp (\Gamma)$ and defined in a standard way.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Pure assertions are modelled by both a context $\Gamma$, and a memory $\sigma\in\mem S$, the satisfaction relation is shown in \Cref{fig:pure-assertions .}
elements.sent-2411-195: The meaning of $\tru$, $\fls$, conjunction, and disjunction are standard.
elements.sent-2411-196: The separating conjunction $P\sep Q$ means that the memory $\sigma \in \mem S$ can be divided into two smaller memories $\sigma_1 \in \mem{S_1}$ and $\sigma_2\in\mem{S_2}$ to satisfy $P$ and $Q$ individually.
elements.sent-2411-197: By the definition of $\uplus$, $S_1$ and $S_2$ must be disjoint.
elements.sent-2411-198: Our logic is an intuitionistic \cite{simon or affine interpretation of separation logic, meaning that information about variables can be discarded; if $\Gamma,\sigma\vDash P$, then $P$ need not describe the entire memory $\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-199: As such, we only require that $\sigma_1 \uplus \sigma_2 \sqsubseteq \sigma$, which we define as $\sigma \sqsubseteq \tau$ iff $\sigma \uplus \sigma' = \tau$ for some $\sigma'$, so $\tau$ could contain more variables than $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-200: We also include a points-to predicate $e\mapsto E$, although it has a slightly different meaning than points-to predicates in the heap model.
elements.sent-2411-201: Here, $e$ does not describe a pointer, but can rather be any concrete expression, and $e\mapsto E$ simply means that the $e$ evaluates to the same value under $\sigma$ as $E$ does under $\Gamma$, allowing us to connect the concrete and logical state.
elements.sent-2411-202: Finally, $E_1\asymp E_2$ allows us to make assertions about logical state, where $\mathord{\asymp \in \{ =, \le, \cdots \}$ ranges over the same comparators that we saw in \Cref{sec:semantics .}}
elements.sent-2411-203: We define the following notation to obtain the set of all memories $\sigma\in \mem S$ that satisfy an assertion $P$, we omit the superscript when we wish to minimize $S$, so that the memories contain only the free variables of $P$: \begin{equation}\sem{P}_\Gamma^S \triangleq \{ \sigma \in \mem S \mid \Gamma, \sigma\vDash P \} \qquad\qquad \sem{P}_\Gamma \triangleq \sem{P}_\Gamma^{fv(P)}\end{equation} Finally, we provide syntactic sugar $e\in S$ for asserting that $e$ belongs to a finite set $S = \{ v_1, \ldots, v_n\}$, and $\own(e)$ for asserting that the resources of $e$ are owned by the current thread. \begin{equation}{e\in S} \triangleq {\exists X. e\mapsto X \sep (X= v_1 \vee \cdots \vee X=v_n)} \qquad\quad \own(e_1, \ldots, e_n) \triangleq \bigsep_{i=1}^n \exists X_i.\ e_i \mapsto X_i\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}{e\in S} \triangleq {\exists X. e\mapsto X \sep (X= v_1 \vee \cdots \vee X=v_n)} \qquad\quad \own(e_1, \ldots, e_n) \triangleq \bigsep_{i=1}^n \exists X_i.\ e_i \mapsto X_i\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-003: \[ \begin{array}{lll} \Gamma, \sigma \vDash \tru \multicolumn{2}{l}{\text{always}} \Gamma, \sigma \vDash \fls \multicolumn{2}{l}{\text{never}} \Gamma,\sigma\vDash P\land Q \text{iff} \Gamma,\sigma\vDash P\quad\text{and}\quad \Gamma,\sigma\vDash Q \Gamma,\sigma\vDash P\lor Q \text{iff} \Gamma,\sigma\vDash P\quad\text{or}\quad \Gamma,\sigma\vDash Q \Gamma, \sigma \vDash P\sep Q \text{iff} \exists \sigma_1,\sigma_2.\quad \sigma_1 \uplus \sigma_2 \sqsubseteq \sigma \quad\text{and}\quad \Gamma,\sigma_1\vDash P \quad\text{and}\quad \Gamma,\sigma_2\vDash Q \Gamma, \sigma \vDash \exists X.\ P \text{iff} \Gamma[X \coloneqq v], \sigma \vDash P \ \text{for some}\ v\in \mathsf{Val} \Gamma, \sigma \vDash e \mapsto E \text{iff} \de{e}_\expr(\sigma) = \de{E}_{\mathsf{LExp}}(\Gamma) \Gamma, \sigma \vDash E_1 \asymp E_2 \text{iff} \de{E_1}(\Gamma) \asymp \de{E_2}(\Gamma) \end{array} \] \caption{Satisfaction relation for pure assertions.} \label{fig:pure-assertions}
elements.sent-2411-204: We now introduce basic definitions related to measure theory and probability spaces.
elements.sent-2411-205: For a more thorough background, refer to \citet{royden1968real or \citet{fremlin2001measure .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: A probability space $\P=\langle\Omega, \mathcal F, \mu\rangle$ consists of a sample space $\Omega$, an event space $\F$, and a probability measure $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-207: For our purposes, the sample space $\Omega\subseteq \mem S$ will consist of memories over a particular set of variables $S$.
elements.sent-2411-208: The event space $\F \subseteq\bb{2 ^\Omega$ gives the events---\ie sets of memories---which are measurable.}
elements.sent-2411-209: It must be a $\sigma$-algebra, meaning that it contains $\emptyset$ and $\Omega$, and it is closed under complementation and countable unions and intersections.
elements.sent-2411-210: The probability measure $\mu \colon \F\to [0,1]$ assigns probabilities to the events in $\F$.
elements.sent-2411-211: For a probability space $\P$, we will use $\Omega_\P$, $\F_\P$, and $\mu_\P$ to refer to its respective parts.
elements.sent-2411-212: We also require probability spaces to be complete, meaning that they contain all events of measure zero.
elements.sent-2411-213: More formally, $\P$ is complete if for any $A \in \F_\P$ such that $\mu_\P(A) = 0$, then $B\in\F_\P$ for all $B\subseteq A$ \cite{royden1968real .}
elements.sent-2411-214: We will often also require the sample space to be the full set of memories $\mem S$ for some $S$.
elements.sent-2411-215: A probability space $\P$ with $\Omega_\P \subset \mem S$ can be completed as follows: $\mathsf{comp (\P) \triangleq \tuple{\mem S, \F, \mu}$ where $\F \triangleq \{ A \cup B \mid A \in \F_\P, B \subseteq \mem S \setminus \Omega_\P \}$ and $\mu(A) \triangleq \mu_\P(A \cap \Omega_\P)$.}
elements.sent-2411-216: We now define an order on probability spaces.
elements.sent-2411-217: As is typical in intuitionistic logic, this order $\P \preceq \Q$ will indicate when $\Q$ contains more information than $\P$.
elements.sent-2411-218: The information can be gained across two dimensions: we can expand the memory footprint to gain more information about other variables, or we can make the event space more granular.
elements.sent-2411-219:
Formally, for $\P$ and $\Q$ such that $\Omega_\P \subseteq \mem S$, we define $\P \preceq \Q$ as follows: \begin{equation}\P \preceq \Q \qquadiff \quad \Omega_\P \subseteq \pi_S(\Omega_\Q) and \quad \F_\P \subseteq \{ \pi_S(A) \mid A \in \F_\Q \}\end{equation}
\begin{equation}and \quad \forall A \in \F_\P.~ \mu_\P(A) = \mu_\Q\left({\bigcup \{B\in \F_\Q\mid \pi_S(B) = A \}}\right)\end{equation} So, $\P\preceq\Q$ iff $\P$ contains a smaller sample space and fewer measurable events, but $\P$ and $\Q$ agree on the probability of events whose projections are measurable in $\P$.
elements.sent-2411-220: For a proper distribution $\mu \in \D(\mem S)$, we will write $\P\preceq \mu$ to mean that $\P \preceq\Q$, where $\Omega_\Q = \mem S$, $\F_\Q = \bb{2 ^{\mem S}$ is the greatest $\sigma$-algebra, and $\mu_\Q(A) = \sum_{\sigma\in A \mu(\sigma)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: We define two more operations on probability spaces, which will help us to give semantics to the separating conjunction and outcome conjunction in \Cref{sec:prob-assert .}
elements.sent-2411-222: The first operation is the independent product $\P\otimes \Q$, which is defined when $\Omega_\P \subseteq \mem S$, $\Omega_\Q\subseteq\mem T$, and $S\cap T=\emptyset$. \begin{equation}\P \otimes \Q \triangleq \tuple{\Omega_\P \sep \Omega_\Q, \{ A \sep B \mid A \in \F_\P, B\in \F_\Q \}, \mu} \quadwhere\quad \mu(A) \triangleq \mu_1(\pi_S(A))\cdot \mu_2(\pi_T(A))\end{equation} Note that this definition is more similar to the initial formulation of \psl \cite{psl (albeit, in a probability space), rather than Lilac and Bluebell, which rely on a theorem stating that independent products are unique \cite[Lemma 2.3]{li2023lilac .}}
elements.sent-2411-223: However, \citet{li2024nominal recently showed that these two models are equivalent.}
elements.sent-2411-224: The next operation is a direct sum for combining disjoint probability spaces \cite[214L]{fremlin2001measure .}

u(i)\cdotp \mu_i(A \cap \Omega_i)\end{equation} The sample space is the union of all the individual sample spaces, the measurable events are those events whose projections into each $\Omega_i$ are measurable according to $\F_i$, and the probability measure is given by a convex sum.
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}\bigoplus_{i \sim \nu} \P_i \triangleq \tuple{\textstyle\biguplus\limits_{i\in I} \Omega_i, \F,\mu}, \quad \F \triangleq \{ A \mid A \subseteq \Omega, \forall i\in I.\ A\cap \Omega_i \in \F_i \}, \quad \mu(A) \triangleq \smashoperator{\sum_{i\in I}} \nu(i)\cdotp \mu_i(A \cap \Omega_i)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-226: The direct sum will be used to give semantics to our outcome conjunction, which provides advantages over the conditioning modalities in Lilac and Bluebell, as we will discuss shortly.
elements.sent-2411-227: Finally, we remark that independent products distribute over direct sums (\Aref{lem:otimes-oplus-dist ): \begin{equation}\textstyle \left(\bigoplus_{i\sim\nu} \P_i \right) \otimes\Q = \bigoplus_{i\sim \nu} (\P_i \otimes \Q)\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-228: We now define probabilistic assertions, which will serve as pre- and postconditions in \pcol triples.
elements.sent-2411-229: The syntax is shown below and the semantics is in \Cref{fig:prob-sem . \begin{equation}\varphi \Coloneqq \top \mid \bot \mid \varphi\land\psi \mid \varphi\lor\psi \mid \smashoperator{\bigoplus_{X\sim d( E)}} \varphi \mid \varphi\sep\psi \mid \sure P\end{equation} The semantics for probabilistic assertions is based on a context $\Gamma\colon \mathsf{LVar}\to\mathsf{Val}$, and a complete probability space $\P = \tuple{\mem S, \F_\P, \mu_\P}$.}
elements.sent-2411-230: The $\top$, $\bot$, conjunction and disjunction assertions have the usual semantics.
elements.sent-2411-231: Next, we have two connectives whose semantics are based on the probability space operations defined in \Cref{sec:measure .}

All the existentially quantified probability spaces are also complete.} \label{fig:prob-sem}

elements.sent-2411-235: The outcome conjunction is similar to the conditioning modalities of Lilac and Bluebell, which use the notation $\bf C$ rather than $\bigoplus$.
elements.sent-2411-236: The key difference is that Lilac and Bluebell do not use a direct sum, but instead require that each $\varphi$ holds in the same $\sigma$-algebra for each $v\in\supp(\mu)$.
elements.sent-2411-237: The upside of that approach is that disjointness of the sample space does not need to be established, making some of the entailment rules simpler.

elements.sent-2411-240: Next, we have the separating conjunction $\varphi\sep \psi$, which uses an independent product to ensure that the probability spaces satisfying $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are independent.
elements.sent-2411-241: The fact that $\P_1\otimes\P_2 \preceq \P$ means that information can be lost so that the events described by $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are independent, even if some correlation exists in $\P$.
elements.sent-2411-242: The almost sure assertion $\sure P$ states that the pure assertion $P$, as described in \Cref{sec:basic-assertions , occurs with probability 1.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Finally, we define syntactic sugar below for a binary outcome conjunction $\varphi \oplus_p \psi$ and $e \sim d(E)$, meaning that $e$ is distributed according to $d(E)$. \begin{equation}\varphi \oplus_p \psi \triangleq \textstyle{\bigoplus_{X \sim \bern p}} (\sure{X = 1} \land \varphi) \vee (\sure{X = 0} \land \psi) \qquad\quad e\sim d(E) \triangleq {\bigoplus_{X\sim d(E)}} \sure{e \mapsto X}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-244: The notion of precision often arises in separation logics, in part to explain when the separating conjunction distributes over other logical connectives, such as the regular conjunction \cite{semanticsep,vafeiadis2011concurrent .}
elements.sent-2411-245: An assertion is precise if it has a unique smallest model.
elements.sent-2411-246: For probabilistic assertions, the formal definition is below.
elements.sent-2411-247: We write $\precise{\vec\varphi}$ if the list of assertions $\vec\varphi$ are all precise.
elements.sent-2411-248: We use precision to guarantee that the separating conjunction distributes over outcome conjunctions.
elements.sent-2411-249: We saw at the end of \Cref{sec:measure that independent products (which model separating conjunctions) distribute over direct sums (which model outcome conjunctions), however the corresponding entailment $\bigoplus_{X\sim d( E) (\varphi\sep \psi) \vdash(\bigoplus_{X\sim d( E) \varphi)\sep \psi$ requires that $\psi$ is satisfied by the same model in each case, which can be guaranteed by forcing $\psi$ to be precise.}}}
elements.sent-2411-250: Precision is important in \pcol, as it guarantees that nondeterminism introduced by the scheduler is not observable in the event space.
elements.sent-2411-251: This is crucial for the \ruleref{Par rule, since we want to be able to conclude that the outcomes of two threads are independent despite any correlation that could be introduced by the particular interleaving order of the threads.}
elements.sent-2411-252: We will see more concrete examples on this in \Cref{sec:logic .}
elements.sent-2411-253: Below, we give a few rules to determine that assertions are precise. \begin{equation}\inferrule{\;}{\precise{\sure P}} \qquad\quad \inferrule{ \precise{\varphi,\psi} }{ \precise{\varphi\sep\psi} } \qquad\quad \inferrule{ \precise\varphi \varphi\Rightarrow\sure{e \mapsto X} }{ \precise{\textstyle\bigoplus_{X\sim d(E)}\varphi} }\end{equation} Almost sure assertions are always precise, since the smallest model is the one where $\sem{P _\Gamma$ occurs with probability 1, and is the smallest measurable set with nonzero probability.}
elements.sent-2411-254: Separating conjunctions are precise if their subcomponents are, which follows from montonicity of the independent product (\Aref{lem:otimes-mono ).}
elements.sent-2411-255: Outcome conjunctions are precise if the inner assertion is precise, and implies that $\sure{e\mapsto X}$ for some program expression $e$, which witnesses how to partition the sample space for the direct sum.
elements.sent-2411-256: For example, $\sure{x \mapsto 1 \oplus_\frac12 \sure{x \in \{0,1\}}$ is not precise, since it is not possible to determine the probability of the event $x=1$, despite it being measurable in one of the sub-probability spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-257: However, $\sure{x \mapsto 1 \sep y\mapsto0 \oplus_\frac12 \sure{x \in \{0,1\} \sep y\mapsto 1}$ is precise, since $y$ witnesses the partition.}

elements.sent-2411-258: We conclude by giving some entailment laws about probabilistic assertions, shown in \Cref{fig:entailment .}
elements.sent-2411-259: Many of these laws echo those of Bluebell, but there are some notable differences highlighting the ways in which our subtle changes to the model result in desirable properties.
elements.sent-2411-260: We first have two rules pertaining to almost sure assertions; weakening can be performed inside of an almost sure assertion\footnote{This rule relies on the fact that probability spaces are complete, so that if $A \in \F_\P$ and $\mu_\P(A) = 1$, then $B \in \F_\P$ for all $B \supseteq A$, allowing us to eschew the concepts of almost-sure equality \cite[Fig. 6]{li2023lilac and almost measurability \cite[Def.. 4.7]{bao2024bluebell .}}}
elements.sent-2411-261: Weakening can also be performed inside of an outcome conjunction, and bound variables can be $\alpha$-renamed as long as the new variable name is fresh.
elements.sent-2411-262: As in Bluebell, the separating conjunction distributes over the outcome conjunction, so that assertions can be moved inside of an outcome conjunction.
elements.sent-2411-263: However, the backward direction of this rule---factoring assertions out of an outcome conjunction---is only supported in Bluebell for almost-sure assertions $\sure P$.
elements.sent-2411-264: In our logic, this operation can be performed for any precise assertion, due to our semantics based on a direct sum.
elements.sent-2411-265: Probabilistic Concurrent Outcome Logic (\pcol) specifications are given as triples of the form $I\vDash\triple\varphi{C}\psi$, where $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are probabilistic assertions (\Cref{sec:prob-assert ), $C\in\mathsf{Cmd}$ (\Cref{fig:syntax ), and $I$ is a basic assertion.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: Roughly speaking, the meaning of these triples is that starting in any distribution satisfying $\varphi$, any distribution resulting from an invariant sensitive execution of $C$ will satisfy $\psi$.
elements.sent-2411-267: Recall from \Cref{sec:linearization that invariant sensitive execution required the invariant states to be drawn from a finite set.}
elements.sent-2411-268: For this reason, we require $I$ to be a finitary basic assertion; formally, we write $\mathsf{finitary (I)$ iff $\sem{I _\Gamma$ is a finite set for any context $\Gamma$.}}
elements.sent-2411-269: We will discuss the implications of this restriction further in \Cref{sec:limitations .}
elements.sent-2411-270: The formal validity definition of \pcol triples is below.
elements.sent-2411-271: As in many separation logics, frame preservation is built into the semantics of the triples \cite{birkedal2007relational,iris ; in addition to quantifying over a probability space $\P$ to satisfy $\varphi$, we also quantify over a probability space $\P_F$, which describes unused resources and is preserved by the program execution.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Note that in the initial distribution $\mu$, the variables in $\P$ and $\P_F$ may be correlated, but the fact that $\P\otimes\P_F\preceq\mu$ means that the correlation will not be measurable.
elements.sent-2411-273: In addition, since we defined probability spaces to operate over memories $\mem S$, without $\bot$, this makes the definition of triples fault avoiding, which is also a standard choice for separation logics \cite{yang2002semantic .}
elements.sent-2411-274: That is, if $I\vDash\triple\varphi{C}\psi$ is valid, then we know that $C$ will not encounter a memory fault starting from a distribution satisfying $\varphi$.

elements.sent-2411-285: It is not hard to imagine situations where the scheduler can introduce correlation between variables.
elements.sent-2411-286: For example, in the following program, the scheduler could choose to schedule the $y \coloneqq 1$ action after the sampling operation is resolved, meaning that it could make $x=y$ with probability 1, a clear correlation. \begin{equation}x \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \fatsemi y \coloneqq 0 \quad\mathlarger{\mathlarger\parallel}\quad y \coloneqq 1\end{equation} As such, the outcomes of the two threads will not be independent after being run concurrently, but rather only observably independent in some restricted event space.
elements.sent-2411-287: By requiring the postconditions for each thread to be precise, we know that the probability of each measurable event must be specified exactly, so that the nondeterminism injected by the scheduler will not be observable, which we prove in \Aref{lem:obs-ind1,lem:par .}

elements.sent-2411-293: The \ruleref{Atom rule opens the invariant by moving it into the triple as an almost sure assertion, as long as the program is a single atomic action $a$.}

elements.sent-2411-295: Next, the \ruleref{Share rule allows a finitary almost sure assertion $I$ to be moved into the invariant.}

elements.sent-2411-297: The \ruleref{Frame rule allows a local specification to be lifted into a larger memory footprint \cite{yang2002semantic .}}
elements.sent-2411-298: The frame $\vartheta$ not only represents a disjoint set of physical resources, but also that those resources are distributed independently from the information about the present program.
elements.sent-2411-299: We next have two conditioning rules, which allow us to deconstruct an outcome conjunction in the precondition to reason about each event individually.
elements.sent-2411-300: Both rules require that the logical variable $X$, that is bound by the outcome conjunction, does not appear free in the invariant $I$, since $X$ is unbound in the premise of the rule.
elements.sent-2411-301: If $X$ is free in $I$, then the rule can be applied after $\alpha$-renaming $X$ in the outcome conjunction (see \Cref{fig:entailment ).}
elements.sent-2411-302: The \ruleref{Cond1 rule requires that $\psi$ dictate the partition of the probability spaces in order to construct a direct sum.}
elements.sent-2411-303: This is done in a similar fashion to rules for establishing precision that we saw in \Cref{sec:prob-assert .}
elements.sent-2411-304: If $\psi$ does not dictate a partition, then the \ruleref{Cond2 rule can instead be used, which requires $\psi$ to be precise and not dependent on $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-305: Although the direct sum semantics of the outcome conjunction imposes some limitations on the conditioning rules, the benefit is that they are fully compositional; the use of conditioning does not impede the application of other rules later in the derivation.
elements.sent-2411-306: This is in contrast with Bluebell's c-wp-swap \cite[\S 5.1]{bao2024bluebell , which requires ownership over all program variables (denoted $\mathsf{own _{\mathbb X}$), thereby precluding most later applications of the frame rule.}}

elements.sent-2411-308: Because of this, we prefer the direct sum semantics for \pcol.
elements.sent-2411-309: The \ruleref{Exists rule allows us to reason underneath existential quantifiers in almost sure assertions.}

elements.sent-2411-335: For example, \citet{bacher2015mergeshuffle showed that shuffling algorithms can be made up to seven times faster through parallelization.}
elements.sent-2411-336: They introduced a divide-and-conquer algorithm---similar to the classic mergesort---in which sub-arrays are shuffled concurrently and then merged using another randomized procedure.
elements.sent-2411-337: A simplified version of their algorithm is shown below. \begin{equation}\left( \;\; \code{shuffle}(x[0, n/2-1]) \;\;{\mathlarger{\parallel}}\;\; \code{shuffle}(x[n/2, n-1]) \;\; \right) \fatsemi \code{merge}(x)\end{equation} Notice that the structure of this program is nearly identical to the batch sampling program above, Indeed, correctness of the concurrent shuffle also relies on the probabilistic independence of the two shuffled sub-arrays, although the proof is out of scope for this paper.
elements.sent-2411-338: Private information retrieval allows a user to fetch data without the database operator learning what data was requested \cite{chor1998private .}
elements.sent-2411-339: We achieve this using the program shown in \Cref{fig:pir .}
elements.sent-2411-340: The \code{fetch procedure takes a bit string query $q$, which indicates which entries of the database $d$ to return.}
elements.sent-2411-341: Those entries are then xor'ed together.
elements.sent-2411-342: Private retrieval is implemented in the \code{privFetch procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-343: The input $x$ is a one-hot bit string, with a 1 in the position of the data being requested and zeros everywhere else.
elements.sent-2411-344: Two queries are then made concurrently.
elements.sent-2411-345: The first one uses a randomly chosen bit string, and the second uses the same random string, but with one bit flipped, corresponding to the entry that the user wants.
elements.sent-2411-346: The final data is then an xor of the two responses.
elements.figure-2411-008: \[ \begin{array}{l} \code{fetch}(q, i, r, d): \quad i \coloneqq 0 \fatsemi r \coloneqq 0 \fse \quad \forloop{n}{\;} \qquad \ift{q[i] = 1}{\;} \qquad\quad r \coloneqq \xor(r, d[i]) \fse \qquad i\coloneqq i+1 \end{array} \qquad\qquad \begin{array}{l} \code{privFetch}(x): \quad q_1 \samp \unif{ \{0, 1\}^n} \fse \quad q_2 \coloneqq \mathsf{xor}(q_1, x) \fse \quad \left( \code{fetch}(q_1, i, r_1, d) \parallel \code{fetch}(q_2, j, r_2, d) \right) \fse \quad r \coloneqq \xor(r_1, r_2) \\\; \end{array} \] \caption{A concurrent private information retrieval protocol.} \label{fig:pir}

elements.sent-2411-354: Polaris is a relational separation logic built on Iris \cite{iris for reasoning about concurrent probabilistic programs \cite{tassarotti2018verifying,polaris .}}
elements.sent-2411-355: Specifications take the form of refinements---complex programs are shown to behave equivalently to idealized ones.
elements.sent-2411-356: Probabilistic analysis is then used to prove the expected behavior of the idealized program, but this analysis is external to the program logic.
elements.sent-2411-357: Compared to Polaris, \pcol has two advantages: probabilistic reasoning is done directly in the logic, and specifications give the full distribution over the program's outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-358: However, unlike Polaris, \pcol does not support advanced forms of separation and ghost state.
elements.sent-2411-359: This logic can be used to lower bound the probability of a single outcome, whereas \pcol is more expressive in its ability to give precise probabilities for many outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-360: Capturing probabilistic independence in separation logic was first explored by \citet{psl , however the resulting Probabilistic Separation Logic (\psl) was limited in its ability to reason about control flow, and the frame rule had stringent side conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-361: DIBI later extended the \psl model to include conditioning, but did not include a full program logic \cite{bao2021bunched .}
elements.sent-2411-362: Lilac built on the two aforementioned logics and used conditioning to improve on \psl's handling of control flow, although without mutable state \cite{li2023lilac .}
elements.sent-2411-363: Lilac also reformulated the notion of separation using probability spaces, making it more expressive.
elements.sent-2411-364: Bluebell added the ability to reason about mutable state, and is also relational so as to capture the joint behavior of multiple programs \cite{bao2024bluebell .}
elements.sent-2411-365: We build on these logics, bringing independence and conditioning into a concurrent setting for the first time, with a modified measure theoretic semantics to support compositional conditioning rules.
elements.sent-2411-366: A second category of probabilistic program logics is built on top of Iris \cite{iris , from which they inherit expressive features, including rich support for ghost state, impredicativity, and invariant reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-367: Additional logics have also been developed for relational reasoning and refinement \cite{gregersen2024almost-sure,ghaselwarter2024approximate,gregersen2024asynchronous .}
elements.sent-2411-368: The tradeoff in these logics is that they focus on a narrow property about programs' probabilistic behaviors, \eg only capturing a bound on an expected cost or probability of a single event.
elements.sent-2411-369: Outcome Separation Logic uses a more primitive form of heap separation (similar to \pcol), but is backed by a denotational model that supports specifications about the distribution of outcomes \cite{zilberstein2024outcome .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-010: Semantics for Probability, Nondeterminism, and Concurrency
elements.sent-2411-370: There is a rich body of research on semantics that combine probabilistic and nondeterministic computation.
elements.sent-2411-371: In this paper, we build on the convex powerset approach \cite{morgan1996refinement , which has been used to define denotational semantics \cite{jifeng1997probabilistic and has well understood equational \cite{mislove2000nondeterminism,mislove2004axioms and domain theoretic properties \cite{tix2009semantic,tix2000convex,tix1999continuous,keimel2017mixed .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-372: We refer to \citet[\S7]{zilberstein2025demonic for an overview of alternative other approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-373: The convex powerset was used as a semantic basis for expectation calculi, which bound the expected values of random variables in probabilistic and nondeterministic programs \cite{wpe,mciver2005abstraction,kaminski .}
elements.sent-2411-374: More recently, Demonic Outcome Logic (\dol) used the convex powerset to develop a logic for reasoning about these programs in terms of their distributions of outcomes, adding more expressive power \cite{zilberstein2025demonic .}
elements.sent-2411-375: However, the convex powerset had not previously intersected with semantics of concurrent programs, which uses partially ordered multisets (pomsets) to record the causality between actions \cite{gischer1988equational,pratt1986modeling .}
elements.sent-2411-376: As discussed in \Cref{sec:semantics , no prior pomset semantics is suitable for use with the convex powerset, as they implicitly reorder probabilistic and nondeterministic choices.}
elements.sent-2411-377: In this paper, we presented Probabilistic Concurrent Outcome Logic (\pcol), which brings together ideas from concurrent and probabilistic separation logics for reasoning about parallel programs in terms of independence and conditioning.
elements.sent-2411-378: This is a significant step in bringing expressive techniques to analysis of probabilistic concurrent programs and required a new semantics to establish soundness.
elements.sent-2411-379: The result is a proof system with familiar inference rules, however the complexity of the underlying metatheory required us to impose several restrictions, limiting the capabilities of \pcol.
elements.sent-2411-380: We now discuss some of those limitations, and how we plan to address them in future work.
elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Unbounded Looping and Almost Sure Termination.
elements.sent-2411-381: The programming language in \Cref{sec:semantics only includes bounded looping.}
elements.sent-2411-382: We imposed this restriction for two reasons.
elements.sent-2411-383: First, there is no known infinitary structure for pomsets with tests, which were needed to capture the semantics of scheduling interleaved with probabilistic sampling.
elements.sent-2411-384: Second, the soundness of \ruleref{Par does not extend to infinite computation in a straightforward way.}
elements.sent-2411-385: We found this limitation acceptable, as no other \psl currently support unbounded iteration \cite{psl,li2023lilac,bao2024bluebell .}
elements.sent-2411-386: However, we plan to augment \pcol with the ability to handle unbounded looping by enriching the pomsets from \Cref{sec:pomset with a dcpo structure, so that infinitary computations can be represented as the limits of their finite approximations \cite{meyer1989pomset,bakker1990metric .}}
elements.sent-2411-387: This will allow us to prove that concurrent programs almost surely terminate---they terminate with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-388: Almost sure termination has been a focus of sequential probabilistic program analysis \cite{mciver2018new,mciver2005abstraction,kaminski .}
elements.sent-2411-389: We plan to introduce the new concept of mutual almost sure termination, in which concurrent loops interacting with shared resources all terminate with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-390: This will allow us to verify synchronization protocols \cite{hart1983termination such as the Dining Philosophers problem \cite{lehmann1981advantages .}}
elements.sent-2411-391: In this paper, we allow invariants that are almost sure statements about shared variables.
elements.sent-2411-392: This helps in establishing soundness of the \ruleref{Par rule, since almost sure assertions are always probabilistically independent from any other assertions.}
elements.sent-2411-393: However, going forward we would like to allow invariants to specify probabilistic properties.
elements.sent-2411-394: This would allow us to verify probabilistic approximate counters \cite{morris1978counting,flajolet1985approximate , where the invariant must specify that the expected value of the approximate count is the true count.}
elements.sent-2411-395: Building on \psl, our resource model uses variables rather than addressed pointers.
elements.sent-2411-396: This simplifies our formulation, as we know that each distribution contains memories over a homogenous set of variables, and therefore we can simply project out separate components of distributions.
elements.sent-2411-397: However, most concurrent programs use pointers, which can be dynamically allocated and alias each other, adding complexity.
elements.sent-2411-398: Further, modern \csl implementations such as Iris \cite{iris,iris1 use resource algebras, so that additional types of physical and logical state can be added.}
elements.sent-2411-399: In particular, ghost state governs the ways in which concurrent threads can modify shared resources.
elements.sent-2411-400: Bluebell already included permissions, which can help to duplicate knowledge about read-only variables \cite{bao2024bluebell , however many other resources are used in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-401: As we saw in \Cref{sec:examples and \Aref{app:examples , \pcol derivations are quite involved---even for small programs---due to the handling of invariants, conditioning, and existential quantification.}}
elements.sent-2411-402: Verification of larger programs would be infeasible with pen-and-paper proofs, therefore we would like to integrate \pcol into Iris \cite{iris,iris1 to mechanize the logic and automate the more tedious details of proofs.}
elements.sent-2411-403: However, our denotational semantics is quite different from Iris's step-indexed model, so significant work would need to be done in order to fit these two logics together.
