elements.sent-2411-467: First we show the condition on sample spaces: \begin{equation}\Omega_{\P \otimes\Q} = \Omega_{\P} \sep \Omega_{\Q} = \{ \sigma\uplus \tau \mid \sigma\in \Omega_{\P}, \tau\in\Omega_{\Q} \} \subseteq \{ \sigma\uplus \tau \mid \sigma\in \pi_S(\Omega_{\P'}), \tau\in\Omega_{\Q} \} = \{ \sigma\uplus \tau \mid \sigma\in \pi_S(\Omega_{\P'}), \tau\in \pi_T(\Omega_{\Q}) \} = \{ \pi_S(\sigma)\uplus \pi_T(\tau) \mid \sigma\in \Omega_{\P'}, \tau\in \Omega_{\Q} \} = \{ \pi_{S\cup T}(\sigma \uplus\tau) \mid \sigma\in \Omega_{\P'}, \tau\in \Omega_{\Q} \} = \pi_{S\cup T}(\Omega_{\P'\otimes\Q})\end{equation} Now we show the condition on $\sigma$-algebras: \begin{equation}\F_{\P \otimes \Q} = \{ A\sep B \mid A \in \F_\P, B \in \F_\Q \} \subseteq \{ A\sep B \mid A \in \pi_S(\F_{\P'}), B \in \F_\Q \} = \{ \pi_S(A)\sep \pi_T(B) \mid A \in \F_{\P'}, B \in \F_\Q \} = \pi_{S\cup T}(\{ A\sep B \mid A \in \F_{\P'}, B \in \F_\Q \}) = \pi_{S\cup T}(\F_{\P' \otimes \Q})\end{equation} Finally, we show the condition on probability spaces: \begin{equation}\mu_{\P\otimes \Q}(A) = \mu_\P(\pi_S(A)) \cdot \mu_\Q(\pi_T(A)) \intertext{Since $\P \preceq\P'$:} = \mu_{\P'}\left(\bigcup \{B \in \F_{\P'} \mid \pi_S(B) =\pi_S(A)\}\right) \cdot \mu_\Q(\pi_T(A)) = \mu_{\P'}\left(\bigcup \{ \pi_{S'}(B) \mid B \in \F_{\P'\otimes \Q},\ \pi_{S\cup T}(B) =A \} \right) \cdot \mu_\Q\left(\bigcup \{ \pi_T(B) \mid B \in \F_{\P'\otimes \Q},\ \pi_{S\cup T}(B) =A \} \right) = \mu_{\P'}\left( \pi_{S'}\left( \bigcup \{ B \in \F_{\P'\otimes \Q} \mid \pi_{S\cup T}(B) =A \} \right)\right) \cdot \mu_\Q\left( \pi_T\left( \bigcup \{ B \in \F_{\P'\otimes \Q} \mid \pi_{S\cup T}(B) =A \} \right)\right) = \mu_{\P'\otimes\Q}\left( \bigcup \{ B \in \F_{\P'\otimes \Q} \mid \pi_{S\cup T}(B) =A \} \right)\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-474: The property on sample spaces is simple: \begin{equation}\Omega_\P = \smashoperator{\bigcup_{i \in \supp(\mu)}} \Omega_{\P} \subseteq \smashoperator{\bigcup_{i\in\supp(\mu)}} \pi_S(comp(\Omega_{\P_i})) = \smashoperator{\bigcup_{i\in\supp(\mu)}} \pi_S(\mem T) = \pi_S\left( \smashoperator[r]{\bigcup_{i\in\supp(\mu)}} \mem T \right) = \pi_S( \Omega_\Q)\end{equation} Next, we verify the property on $\sigma$-algebras. \begin{equation}\F_\P = \bigcap_{i\in I} \F_\P \subseteq \bigcap_{i\in I} \{ \pi_S(A) \mid A \in \F_{comp(\P_i)} \} = \{ \pi_S(A) \mid \forall i\in I.\ A \in \F_{comp(\P_i)} \} \intertext{Note that the completion adds information about events outside of $\Omega_{\P_i}$, so if $A$ is in all of the $\F_{comp(\P_i)}$ sets, then its projection into each $\Omega_{\P_i}$ must be in $\F_{\P_i}$.} = \{ \pi_S(A) \mid A \subseteq \Omega_Q, \forall i.\ A \cap \Omega_{\P_i} \in \F_{\P_i} \} = \{ \pi_S(A) \mid A \in \F_\Q \}\end{equation} Finally, we show the property on probability measures. \begin{equation}\mu_\P(A) = \smashoperator{\sum_{i\in \supp(\mu)}} \mu(i) \cdot \mu_\P(A) = \smashoperator{\sum_{i\in \supp(\mu)}} \mu(i) \cdot \mu_{comp(\P_i)}\left( \bigcup \{B \in \F_{comp(\P_i)} \mid \pi_S(B) = A \} \right) \intertext{The completion assigns zero probability to events outside of $\Omega_{\P_i}$, so removing the completion and projecting into $\Omega_{\P_i}$ will yield the same value.} = \smashoperator{\sum_{i\in \supp(\mu)}} \mu(i) \cdot \mu_{\P_i}\left( \left(\bigcup \{ B \in \F_{\Q} \mid \pi_S(B) = A \}\right) \cap \Omega_{\P_i} \right) = \mu_\Q\left( \bigcup \{ B\in\F_\Q \mid \pi_S(B) = A \} \right)\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-619: Although randomization has long been used in distributed computing, formal methods for reasoning about probabilistic concurrent programs have lagged behind.
elements.sent-2411-620: No existing program logics can express specifications about the full distributions of outcomes resulting from programs that are both probabilistic and concurrent.
elements.sent-2411-621: To address this, we introduce Probabilistic Concurrent Outcome Logic (\pcol), which incorporates ideas from concurrent and probabilistic separation logics into Outcome Logic to introduce new compositional reasoning principles.
elements.sent-2411-622: At its core, \pcol reinterprets the rules of Concurrent Separation Logic in a setting where separation models probabilistic independence, so as to compositionally describe joint distributions over variables in concurrent threads.
elements.sent-2411-623: Reasoning about outcomes also proves crucial, as case analysis is often necessary to derive precise information about threads that rely on randomized shared state.
elements.sent-2411-624: We demonstrate \pcol on a variety of examples, including to prove almost sure termination for unbounded loops.
elements.sent-2411-625: Concurrent algorithms can be made more efficient using randomization \cite{rabin1980n-process,rabin1982choice,morris1978counting and some distributed synchronization problems have no deterministic solution \cite{fischer1985impossibility,lehmann1981advantages .}}
elements.sent-2411-626: But despite the prevalance of randomization in concurrent computing over the last several decades, formal methods for these types of programs are limited.
elements.sent-2411-627: The mixture of computational effects in probabilistic concurrent programs is a major source of difficulty in developing such techniques; random choice is introduced by sampling operations and nondeterminism arises from scheduling the concurrent threads.
elements.sent-2411-628: These two computational effects do not compose in standard ways \cite{varacca_winskel_2006 , so even just describing the semantics of such programs requires specialized models \cite{jifeng1997probabilistic,mciver2005abstraction,zilberstein2025denotational .}}
elements.sent-2411-629: However, while \dol's approach for describing this space of possible distributions provides a basis for \pcol, reasoning about the nondeterminism that arises from concurrent scheduling is substantially more complicated than reasoning about a choice operator.
elements.sent-2411-630: With a choice operator, nondeterminism is localized to the points where the operator is used, whereas in a concurrent program, every step can potentially involve nondeterminism from thread interleaving.
elements.sent-2411-631: Probabilistic Separation Logics (\psl) use the notions of independence and conditioning to reason about the interaction between randomness and control flow \cite{psl,li2023lilac,bao2025bluebell,bao2021bunched,bao2022separation,yan2025combining .}
elements.sent-2411-632: However, as we will see in \Cref{sec:overview , such a combination is challenging to achieve because the metatheories of the two logics are highly specialized to their respective domains, and a direct combination of their rules would not be sound.}
elements.sent-2411-633: This paper develops the metatheory in a more complex semantic domain, where concurrency and probabilistic computation can coexist.
elements.sent-2411-634: As a result, \pcol is the first logic to combine all of the following features:.
elements.sent-2411-635: Similar to Concurrent Separation Logic (\csl), this relies on separation---as long as two threads operate on their own portions of memory, they cannot interfere with each other.
elements.sent-2411-636: In addition, shared state is handled via resource invariants, properties about shared state that remain true at every step.
elements.sent-2411-637: In a probabilistic context, separation of memory footprints is not sufficient; while it can tell us how the local variables of each thread are distributed, it does not give us the joint distribution over the entire global memory.
elements.sent-2411-638: In response, we use a \psl-style model where separation additionally models probabilistic independence \cite{psl .}
elements.sent-2411-639: Going beyond prior work, our parallel composition rule guarantees that the scheduler cannot introduce any probabilistic correlation between the local states of each thread.
elements.sent-2411-640: When concurrent threads depend on randomized shared state, it is often necessary to do case analysis over the possible values of that shared state in order to capture the probabilistic correlation between the threads (\eg see \Cref{sec:overview-outcomes ).}
elements.sent-2411-641: In the style of Demonic Outcome Logic (\dol) \cite{zilberstein2025demonic , \pcol supports compositional reasoning about the outcomes generated via both probabilistic branching and the nondeterministic behavior of the scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-642: But unlike \dol---which does not support separation---case analysis must be done with care, as it can invalidate the independence guarantees of the \ruleref{Frame rule.}
elements.sent-2411-643: Compared to prior outcome logics, the outcome conjunction of \pcol has a new measure theoretic foundation.
elements.subsubsection-2411-018: Unbounded Looping and Almost Sure Termination
elements.sent-2411-644: Our logic includes rules for establishing almost sure termination---termination with probability 1---for unbounded loops.
elements.sent-2411-645: This goes beyond the capabilities of all prior separation logics that model separation with probabilistic independence, which either have only bounded looping constructs (\eg for loops) or require loops to always terminate (which must be established externally to the logic).
elements.sent-2411-646: Unbounded looping is important in randomized concurrent programs, as such programs often only achieve the desired distribution of outcomes in the limit, and not after a bounded number of steps (\eg \Cref{sec:von-neumann ).}
elements.sent-2411-647: We begin in \Cref{sec:overview with an overview of the technical challenges and design of \pcol.}
elements.sent-2411-648: Next, in \Cref{sec:semantics we outline the programming language and semantic model that we will use.}
elements.sent-2411-649: The logic and inference rules are defined in \Cref{sec:model,sec:logic .}
elements.sent-2411-650: We demonstrate the capabilities of \pcol on four case studies in \Cref{sec:examples .}
elements.sent-2411-651: Finally, we conclude by discussing related work and future directions in \Cref{sec:related,sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-652: Omitted proofs and details are given in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-653: In this paper, we develop a logic for verifying the correctness of randomized concurrent imperative programs, written in a language that includes control flow operations (if statements and while loops), parallel composition $C_1 \parallel C_2$, and random sampling $x \samp d$.
elements.sent-2411-654: A major hurdle in concurrency analysis is that the semantics is non-compositional; two programs can have completely different behavior when run in parallel than they do when run in isolation.
elements.sent-2411-655: Enumerating all possible interleavings of the threads is not a viable strategy, so abstractions must be introduced for sound compositional analysis.
elements.sent-2411-656: In this section, we explore the mechanisms that enable compositional reasoning about probabilistic concurrent programs in \pcol, including the challenges that arise with shared state.
elements.subsection-2411-018: Concurrent Separation Logic meets Probabilistic Separation Logic
elements.sent-2411-657: Concurrent Separation Logic (\csl) achieves compositionality via separation \cite{csl,brookes2004semantics ---if two threads act on disjoint memory regions, then their behavior will not change when run in parallel.}
elements.sent-2411-658: This idea is encapsulated by the \hyperlink{rule:par1 {Par rule, where the separating conjunction $\varphi\sep\psi$ means that the machines's memory cells can be divided to satisfy $\varphi$ and $\psi$ individually.}}
elements.sent-2411-659: In addition, the \hyperlink{rule:frame1 {Frame rule guarantees that threads cannot interfere with memory outside of their local state.}}
elements.sent-2411-660: For example, if we flip two coins and store the results in the variables $x$ and $y$, then the respective variables will be distributed according to Bernoulli distributions with parameter $\frac12$, as shown in the following specifications, where $\sure P$ means that $P$ holds with probability 1 (almost surely), ${x\mapsto -}$ means that the current thread has permission to read and write $x$, and $x \sim d$ means that $x$ is distributed according to $d$. \begin{equation}\triple{\sure{x\mapsto -}}{x \samp \bern{\tfrac12}}{x \sim \bern{\tfrac12}} \qquad\quad \triple{\sure{y\mapsto -}}{y \samp \bern{\tfrac12}}{y \sim \bern{\tfrac12}}\end{equation} Composing these programs in parallel, we would ideally want to derive a specification that dictates not only how $x$ and $y$ are distributed, but also their joint distribution.
elements.sent-2411-661: In this case, $x$ and $y$ are probabilistically independent, meaning that each outcome (\eg $x$ and $y$ are both 1) occurs with probability $\frac12\cdot\frac12=\frac14$.
elements.sent-2411-662: Thus, it is natural to consider using the interpretation of separating conjunction proposed in Probabilistic Separation Logic (\psl) \cite{psl , in which $\varphi\sep\psi$ states that the events described by $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are probabilistically independent.}
elements.sent-2411-663: By treating separation as both disjointness of memory and probabilistic independence, one might hope to validate a probabilistic interpretation of the \hyperlink{rule:par1 {Par rule, with which we could derive the following specification. \begin{equation}\triple{\sure{x\mapsto -} \sep \sure{y\mapsto -}}{x \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \parallel y \samp \bern{\tfrac12}}{x \sim \bern{\tfrac12} \sep y \sim \bern{\tfrac12}}\end{equation} Intuitively, the probabilistic interpretation of \hyperlink{rule:par1 {Par in this instance is justifiable because $C_1$ and $C_2$ execute without interaction, so there is no correlation between their random behaviors.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-664: This lack of correlation is due to that fact that there is no shared state, and therefore the nondeterminism introduced by the interleaving behavior of the scheduler is not observable in any way.
elements.sent-2411-665: Bigger challenges arise when the threads do share state, making the nondeterministic behavior of the scheduler observable.
elements.sent-2411-666: More specifically, \csl provides the following inference rules: a more general \hyperlink{rule:par2 {Par rule allows the invariant resources to be used in both threads; the \hyperlink{rule:atom2 {Atom rule opens the invariant, as long as the program is a single atomic command $a$; and \hyperlink{rule:share2 {Share allocates an invariant that is true before and after the program execution.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-667: In the remainder of this section, we will see a few such representative scenarios, and how they are handled in \pcol.
elements.subsection-2411-019: Handling Randomized Shared State with Outcome Logic
elements.sent-2411-668: When shared state is randomized, compositional reasoning about outcomes becomes essential.
elements.sent-2411-669: As an example, consider the following program, where $x$ and $y$ read from the shared randomized variable $z$ in different threads. \begin{equation}\label{eq:conditioning} z \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \fatsemi ( x\coloneqq z \parallel y \coloneqq 1-z )\end{equation} Here, $x$ and $y$ are clearly not independently distributed, since both are derived from $z$.
elements.sent-2411-670: So it seems dubious that \ruleref{Par could be used to reason about this program.}
elements.sent-2411-671: The key observation is that $x$ and $y$ are conditionally independent on $z$, so we can compositionally reason about the threads by first breaking down the outcomes of the sampling operation such that $z$ is deterministic in each case.
elements.sent-2411-672: We draw inspiration from the logics \DIBI \cite{bao2021bunched , Lilac~\cite{li2023lilac , and Bluebell~\cite{bao2025bluebell , which include constructs to reason about conditioning, but which are not sufficient for the kind of analysis needed here.}}}
elements.sent-2411-673: To support both case analysis and parallel composition, in \pcol we introduce an outcome conjunction $\bigoplus_{X\sim d}\varphi$, which binds a new logical variable $X$ that can be referenced in $\varphi$, and is distributed according to $d$, \eg $z \sim \bern{\frac12}$ is syntactic sugar for $\bigoplus_{Z \sim\bern{\frac12 \sure{z\mapsto Z}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-674: Outcome conjunctions feature in prior logics \cite{outcome,zilberstein2025outcome,zilberstein2025demonic,zilberstein2024outcome , but in this paper we use a new measure theoretic foundation based on direct sums \cite{fremlin2001measure , which interacts well with separation, and represents conditional probabilities via Bayes' Law.}}
elements.sent-2411-675: As a result, the \ruleref{Split rule---shown below---is admissible in \pcol, which is critical for concurrency reasoning.\footnote{Our approach of using direct sums is not without tradeoffs: \pcol is fundamentally discrete, whereas other logics, notably Lilac~\cite{li2023lilac , support continuous distributions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-676: Despite this restriction, our examples will illustrate the expressive power of the logic, as many uses of randomization in concurrent and distributed programs only require discrete distributions.
elements.sent-2411-677: See \Cref{sec:related for a deeper comparison with other logics. \begin{equation}\ruledef{Split}{ I\vdash\triple{\varphi}C{\psi} }{\textstyle I\vdash\triple{\bigoplus_{X\sim d}\varphi}C{\bigoplus_{X\sim d}\psi} }\end{equation} In the premise of \ruleref{Split , $X$ is unbound, and therefore it is implicitly universally quantified.}}
elements.sent-2411-678: So, the rule allows us to partition the sample space according to $d$, reason about the program as if $X$ is a deterministic value, and then once again bind $X$ under an outcome conjunction in the conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-679: Returning to Example (\ref{eq:conditioning ), by considering each possible outcome of the sampling operation, we end up in a situation where $z$ is deterministic, and therefore the \hyperlink{rule:par2 {Par rule can apply.}}}
elements.sent-2411-680: Indeed, the \ruleref{Split rule is the missing piece that we need.}
elements.sent-2411-681: After executing the sampling operation, we get the assertion $(z \sim \bern{\frac12 )\sep\sure{x\mapsto - \sep y\mapsto -}$, which is equivalent to $\bigoplus_{Z\sim\bern{\frac12 \sure{z \mapsto Z \sep x\mapsto-\sep y\mapsto-}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-682: So, to analyze the parallel composition, all we have to do is apply \ruleref{Split to make $z$ deterministic, and then allocate the invariant $z \mapsto Z$, stating that $z$ has some fixed---but universally quantified---value.}
elements.sent-2411-683: The derivation is sketched in \Cref{fig:conditioning-deriv , and shown fully in \Aref{app:cond-ind .}}
elements.sent-2411-684: Ultimately, we conclude that $x$ and $y$ are independent inside the scope of the $\bigoplus_{Z\sim\bern{\frac12 }$---where they are deterministic---while still recording exactly how they are probabilistically correlated.}
elements.sent-2411-685: This pattern arises frequently, \eg in \Cref{sec:shuffle , where we prove the correctness of a concurrent shuffling algorithm in \pcol.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \[ \inferrule*[right=\ruleref{Split}]{ \inferrule*[Right=\ruleref{Share}]{ \inferrule*[Right=\ruleref{Par}]{ \inferrule*{}{ z\mapsto Z \vdash\triple{\sure{x\mapsto-}}{x \coloneqq z}{\sure{x\mapsto Z}} } \quad \inferrule*{}{ z\mapsto Z \vdash\triple{\sure{y\mapsto-}}{y \coloneqq 1-z}{\sure{y\mapsto 1-Z}} } }{ z\mapsto Z \vdash\triple{\sure{x\mapsto-\sep y\mapsto-}}{x \coloneqq z \parallel y \coloneqq 1-z}{\sure{x\mapsto Z\sep y\mapsto1-Z}} } }{ \vdash\triple{\sure{z \mapsto Z\sep x\mapsto-\sep y\mapsto-}}{x \coloneqq z \parallel y \coloneqq 1-z}{\sure{x\mapsto Z\sep y\mapsto1-Z}} } }{ \vdash\triple{\textstyle\bigoplus_{Z\sim\bern{\frac12}} \sure{z \mapsto Z \sep x\mapsto-\sep y\mapsto-}}{x \coloneqq z \parallel y \coloneqq 1-z}{\textstyle\bigoplus_{Z\sim\bern{\frac12}} \sure{x\mapsto Z\sep y\mapsto1-Z}} } \] \caption{Sketch of the derivation for program (\ref{eq:conditioning}).} \label{fig:conditioning-deriv}
elements.sent-2411-686: In the previous section, we saw a new form of compositional reasoning, but it was limited to scenarios where the shared state could be made deterministic via case analysis.
elements.sent-2411-687: In other words, the nondeterministic scheduling order could not affect the outcome of the program.
elements.sent-2411-688: The effects of scheduling become observable when threads mutate shared state, because different interleavings can cause the shared variables to take on different values throughout the program execution.
elements.sent-2411-689: In fact, treating the scheduler adversarially, shared state provides opportunities for the scheduler to introduce probabilistic correlations in unexpected ways.
elements.sent-2411-690: For example, in the following program, the scheduler can force $x$ and $y$ to be equal by first executing the sampling operation; then, with the value of $x$ fixed, choosing an order for the writes to $y$ so that $y \coloneqq 1-x$ is first, and $y \coloneqq x$ is last. \begin{equation}\label{eq:coinflip-game} \left( x \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \fatsemi y \coloneqq 0 \right) \;\; \mathlarger{\mathlarger\parallel} \;\; y\coloneqq 1\end{equation} In that case, $x$ and $y$ are both distributed according to $\bern{\frac12}$, but are certainly not independently distributed.
elements.sent-2411-691: To ensure that these correlations do not invalidate the \ruleref{Par rule in \pcol, we additionally require the postconditions of each thread to be precise (\Cref{def:precise ), essentially meaning that they exactly determine the probability of each event.}}
elements.sent-2411-692: Any pure assertion $\sure P$ is precise---$P$ occurs with probability exactly $1$---as is $x\sim d$, since $d$ dictates the probability of $\sure{x \mapsto v}$ for all $v$.
elements.sent-2411-693: Given that, $(x\sim \bern{\frac12 )\sep \sure{y \in \{0, 1\}}$ is a valid---and precise---postcondition for (\ref{eq:coinflip-game ).}}
elements.sent-2411-694: This may be surprising; as we just saw, $x$ and $y$ will not necessarily be independently distributed.
elements.sent-2411-695: However, \pcol uses a probability-space-as-a-resource model of separation, first explored by \citet{li2023lilac , to make separation more flexible.}
elements.sent-2411-696: That is, we care only about independence of events and not samples; if we can only measure the probability of something that holds with probability 1---\ie $\sure{y\in\{0,1\}}$, and nothing finer like $\sure{y\mapsto 0}$ and $\sure{y\mapsto 1}$---then separation considers only disjointness of memory and not independence, since the probabilistic correlation is not measurable.
elements.sent-2411-697: While it is difficult to determine the exact set of possible joint distributions, \pcol neatly abstracts away those semantic considerations using simple syntactic checks on the postcondition.
elements.subsection-2411-021: Weak Separation and Case Analysis over Shared State
elements.sent-2411-698: Examples (\ref{eq:conditioning ) and (\ref{eq:coinflip-game ) illustrate how \pcol combines both the disjointness and independence interpretations of separation to obtain a probabilistic \hyperlink{rule:par1 {Par rule.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-699: However, in some cases, the combination of disjointness and independence is too strong and does not hold, because the scheduler can induce correlations between concurrent threads through shared state.
elements.sent-2411-700: While we can sometimes coarsen what is measurable to recover independence---as we did with $y$ in (\ref{eq:coinflip-game )---this is not always possible.}
elements.sent-2411-701: In response, \pcol uses a second form of separating conjunction, which we call weak separation, written $\varphi \osep \psi$, which only requires that $\varphi$ and $\psi$ hold for disjoint state, and need not be probabilistically independent.
elements.sent-2411-702: This allows us to still recover some of the benefits of separation logic for reasoning about disjointness, even when we cannot expect independence to hold.
elements.sent-2411-703: To see an example, consider a thread running the following expression, in which control flow depends on a variable $x$ that is part of shared state and may be written by another thread: \begin{equation}C \quad \triangleq \quad x'\coloneqq x \fatsemi {\iftf{x'}{z \samp \bern{\tfrac12}}{z \coloneqq 1}}\end{equation} To analyze the program above, we need to do case analysis on the value of $x$ at the moment that it is read.
elements.sent-2411-704: Then, we conclude that regardless of $x$'s value, $z$ is distributed according to a Bernoulli distribution with parameter at least $\frac12$, motivating the following triple. \begin{equation}\label{eq:weak-triple} x\mapsto 0 \vee x\mapsto 1 \vdash\triple{\sure{z\mapsto- \sep x' \mapsto -}}{C}{ \exists X\ge \tfrac12.\ z \sim \bern{X}}\end{equation} This triple is indeed valid on its own, but it is not compatible with the \hyperlink{rule:frame1 {Frame rule that we saw in \Cref{sec:overview-csl-psl .}}}
elements.sent-2411-705: To see why, consider the extended program \begin{equation}(y \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \fatsemi C) \parallel (x \coloneqq 1)\end{equation} where $x$ is initially $0$.
elements.sent-2411-706: In the left thread, after the $y \samp \bern{\tfrac12}$, if we apply \hyperlink{rule:frame1 {Frame with $\vartheta = y \sim \bern{\frac12}$ and use the triple for $C$ in (\ref{eq:weak-triple ), we would get the postcondition $(\exists X\ge \tfrac12.\ z\sim \bern{X ) \sep (y\sim\bern{\frac12 )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-707: This is not valid because the scheduler can force $y$ and $z$ to be correlated by using the value of $y$ to influence the value of $x$.\footnote{For the interested reader: if the scheduler makes $x$ equal to $y$, then $x$ must also be uniformly distributed, and so $z \sim \bern{\frac12}$ with probability $\frac12$ and $z\mapsto 1$ with probability $\frac12$, which implies that $z \sim \bern{\frac34}$.}
elements.sent-2411-708: But clearly, $(z \sim\bern{\frac34 )\sep(y\sim\bern{\frac12 )$ does not hold, since whenever $y = 0$, then $z=1$, and so the probability of $\sure{y\mapsto 0\sep z\mapsto 1}$ is $\frac12$, which is not equal to $\frac12\cdot\frac34$.}}
elements.sent-2411-709: As we show in \Cref{sec:structural , case analysis on nondeterministic state causes triples to only be weakly frame preserving, meaning that only a variant of the \ruleref{Frame rule using weak separation $\varphi \osep \psi$ can be used.}}
elements.sent-2411-710: Under the right conditions, strong frame preservation can be restored, as shown in \Cref{sec:ast-rules,sec:entropy-mixer,sec:von-neumann .}
elements.sent-2411-711: Having now given an overview of \pcol's features, we begin the technical development in \Cref{sec:semantics by outlining a denotational model for probabilistic concurrent programs.}

elements.section-2411-012: A Probabilistic and Concurrent Programming Language
elements.figure-2411-010:
egin{equation}Cmd 
i C \Coloneqq \skp \mid C_1  atsemi C_2 \mid C_1 \parallel C_2 \mid \iftf b{C_1}{C_2} \mid \whl{b}{C} \mid a \act

i e \Coloneqq x \mid v \mid b \mid e[e'] \mid [e_1, \ldots, e_n] \mid e_1 + e_2 \mid e_1 \cdot e_2 \mid \cdots\end{equation} \caption{Syntax of a probabilistic concurrent language, where $x\in\mathsf{Var}$, $v \in \mathsf{Val}$, and $\mathord{\asymp} \in \{ =, \le, <, \ldots \}$.} \label{fig:syntax}
elements.sent-2411-715: Programs are represented as commands $C\in\mathsf{Cmd}$, which consist of no-ops ($\skp$), sequential composition ($C_1\fatsemi C_2$), parallel composition ($C_1\parallel C_2$), if statements, while loops, and actions $a\in\act$.
elements.sent-2411-716: Actions can perform probabilistic sampling operations $x \samp d(e)$, where $d\in\mathsf{Dist}$ is a discrete probability distribution with the expression $e$ as a parameter.
elements.sent-2411-717: We include three types of distributions---Bernoulli distributions $\bern p$, assigning probability $p$ to $1$ and probability $1-p$ to $0$; geometric distributions $\geo p$, assigning probability $(1-p)^n p$ to each $n \in\mathbb N$; and uniform distributions $\unif e$ where $e$ evaluates to a finite set or list of values $[v_1, \ldots, v_n]$, each having probability $1/n$.
elements.sent-2411-718: The language also has deterministic assignments $x\coloneqq e$, where $e$ is an expression.
elements.sent-2411-719: The addition of random sampling presents several challenges in defining a suitable semantics, as typical approaches do not properly account for the interactions between probabilistic actions and the nondeterministic scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-720: As such, this language must be interpreted in a domain that supports both randomization and nondeterminism, which we briefly describe in \Cref{sec:convex .}
elements.sent-2411-721: We then define the semantics of atomic actions and tests in \Cref{sec:actions .}
elements.sent-2411-722: For commands, we use the recently introduced Pomsets with Formulae \cite{zilberstein2025denotational model, which augments typical compositional denotational techniques for concurrent semantics \cite{gischer1988equational,pratt1986modeling with the ability to record deterministic branching behavior, so that, \eg if statements that depend on random events can be interpreted properly.}}
elements.sent-2411-723: We briefly introduce this model in \Cref{sec:pomset .}
elements.subsection-2411-022: Preliminaries: Memories and the Convex Powerset
elements.sent-2411-724: Programs must be interpreted in a domain that supports both probabilistic and nondeterministic computation.
elements.sent-2411-725: The difficulty is that typical representations of probabilistic computation (distributions) do not compose well with nondeterminism (powersets) \cite{varacca_winskel_2006,zwart2019no .}
elements.sent-2411-726: We instead use the convex powerset $\C$ in our denotational semantics, which we describe in this section.
elements.sent-2411-727: A memory $\sigma \in \mem S \triangleq S\to \mathsf{Val}$ is a mapping from a finite set of variables $S \subseteq_\fin \mathsf{Var}$ to values, where $\mathsf{Val}$ consist of integers, rationals, and lists.
elements.sent-2411-728: The disjoint union $\mathord{\uplus}\colon \mem S \to \mem T \to \mem{S\cup T}$ combines two memories as long as $S\cap T=\emptyset$, and a similar operation $A\sep B$ is defined on sets of memories $A\subseteq \mem S$ and $B\subseteq \mem T$.
elements.sent-2411-729: A\sep B \triangleq \{ \sigma \uplus \tau \mid \sigma\in A, \tau \in B \} \end{mathpar The notation $A\sep B$ is reminiscent of the separating conjunction \cite{bi , and indeed we will use it in \Cref{sec:logic to define the separating conjunction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-730: We define projections $\pi_S \colon \mem T \to \mem{S\cap T}$ as $\pi_S(\sigma)(x) \triangleq \sigma(x)$ if $x\in S$.
elements.sent-2411-731: Expressions are interpreted in the usual way with $\de{e _\expr \colon \mem S \to \mathsf{Val}$ as long as $\free(e) \subseteq S$, if not then $\de{e _\expr(\sigma)$ is undefined.}}
elements.sent-2411-732: The same is true for tests and $\de{b _\test\colon \mem S\to \mathbb B$ (where $\mathbb{B = \{0,1\}$) is defined if $\free(b) \subseteq S$.}}

elements.sent-2411-739: The convex combination of two distributions is defined as $\mu \oplus_p\nu = p\cdot\mu + (1-p)\cdot \nu$.
elements.sent-2411-740: A set of distributions $S\subseteq \D(X \cup \{\bot\})$ is convex if it is closed under convex combinations: $(\mu \oplus_p \nu) \in S$ for every $\mu,\nu\in S$ and $p\in[0,1]$.

elements.sent-2411-757: The invariant-sensitive model is a family of semantic functions for actions, indexed by a semantic invariant: $\de{a _\act^\I \colon \mem S \to \C(\mem S)$, where $\I \subseteq_\fin \mem T$ and $T\subseteq S$. \begin{equation}\de{a}^\I_\act \triangleq (check^\I)^\dagger \circ \de{a}_\act^\dagger \circ (replace^\I)^\dagger \circ check^\I\end{equation} \begin{equation}check^\I(\sigma) \triangleq \left\{ {ll} \eta(\sigma) if~ \pi_T(\sigma) \in \I \bot_\C if~ \pi_T(\sigma) \notin \I  \right. \qquad replace^\I(\sigma) = \bignd_{\tau \in \I} \eta(\pi_S(\sigma) \uplus \tau)\end{equation} In the invariant sensitive semantics, $\I$ is first checked to ensure that the current state satisfies the invariant.}}
elements.sent-2411-758: Next, a new valid state (or distribution thereof) is chosen to replace the current one, simulating a parallel thread which may alter the shared state at any step.
elements.sent-2411-759: The standard action semantics is then executed, followed by another check to ensure that the invariant still holds.
elements.sent-2411-760: If the invariant is ever violated, then $\bot_\C$ is returned to indicate that the execution is faulty.

elements.sent-2411-783: We define the following notation to obtain the set of all memories $\sigma\in \mem S$ that satisfy an assertion $P$, we omit the superscript when we wish to minimize $S$, so that the memories contain only the free variables of $P$: \begin{equation}\sem{P}_\Gamma^S \triangleq \{ \sigma \in \mem S \mid \Gamma, \sigma\vDash P \} \qquad\qquad \sem{P}_\Gamma \triangleq \sem{P}_\Gamma^{\free(P)}\end{equation} Finally, we provide syntactic sugar for restricting the domain of existential quantifiers, asserting membership in a set, and asserting that the resources of $e$ are owned by the current thread.
elements.sent-2411-784: We now introduce basic definitions from measure theory needed to define probabilistic separation.

elements.sent-2411-786: We require probability spaces to be complete, meaning that they contain all events of measure zero.
elements.sent-2411-787: A probability space $\P$ with $\Omega_\P \subseteq \mem S$ can be extended as follows: $\comp(\P) \triangleq \tuple{\mem S, \F, \mu}$ where $\F \triangleq \{ A \subseteq \mem{S \mid A \cap \Omega_\P \in \F_\P \}$ and $\mu(A) \triangleq \mu_\P(A \cap \Omega_\P)$.}

\begin{equation}and \quad \forall A \in \F_\P.~ \mu_\P(A) = \mu_\Q\left({\bigcup \{B\in \F_\Q\mid \pi_S(B) = A \}}\right)\end{equation} So, $\P\preceq\Q$ iff $\P$ contains smaller sample and event spaces, but $\P$ and $\Q$ agree on the probability of events whose projections are measurable in $\P$.

elements.sent-2411-812: Next, we have two variations of the separating conjunction, parameterized by a mode $m\in\{\st, \wk\}$.
elements.sent-2411-813: Strong separation (mode $\st$) is the interpretation of separation that requires probabilistic independence and separation of variables, whereas weak separation (mode $\wk$) does not require independence, and only separates the variables.
elements.sent-2411-814: As we explained in \Cref{sec:overview , weak separation is needed when case analysis over nondeterministic shared state leaves us in a scenario where strong frame preservation does not hold.}
elements.sent-2411-815: Semantically, the difference is captured by the combinator operation $\diamond_m$, with strong separation using an independent product and weak separation simply requiring that the marginal probability spaces are correct. \begin{equation}\P_1 \diamond_\st \P_2 \triangleq \{ \P_1 \otimes \P_2 \} \P_1 \diamond_\wk \P_2 \triangleq \{ \P \mid \P_1 = \pi_U(\P), \P_2 = \pi_V(\P) \}\end{equation} where $\Omega_{\P_1 = \mem{U}$ and $\Omega_{\P_2 = \mem V$ and $U\cap V = \emptyset$.}}
elements.sent-2411-816: Clearly, $\P_1 \diamond_\st\P_2 \subseteq \P_1\diamond_\wk\P_2$, which immediately gives us that $\varphi \sep_\st \psi \Rightarrow \varphi \osep \psi$.
elements.sent-2411-817: Strong separation will be used more commonly, so we will drop the subscript there and write $\sep$ to mean $\sep_\st$.
elements.sent-2411-818: Finally, the almost sure assertion $\sure P$ states that the pure assertion $P$, as described in \Cref{sec:basic-assertions , occurs with probability 1.}
elements.sent-2411-819: We also define syntactic sugar below for a binary outcome conjunction $\oplus_E$, a bounded binary outcome conjunction $\oplus_{\ge E}$, and expressions distributed according to some distribution $e \sim d(E)$. \begin{equation}\varphi \oplus_E \psi \triangleq {\bigoplus_{X \sim \bern E}} (\sure{X = 1} \sep \varphi) \vee (\sure{X = 0} \sep \psi) \qquad\quad e\sim d(E) \triangleq {\bigoplus_{X\sim d(E)}} \sure{e \mapsto X}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\varphi \oplus_{\ge E} \psi \triangleq \exists X.\ \sure{X \ge E} \sep (\varphi \oplus_X \psi)\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-025: Convex and Precise Assertions and Entailment Laws
elements.sent-2411-820: As we mentioned in \Cref{sec:overview , the parallel composition rule of \pcol requires the postcondition from each thread to be precise, so that any correlations introduced via concurrent scheduling are not measurable.}
elements.sent-2411-821: We now define precision formally in terms of probability spaces.
elements.sent-2411-822: Below, we give a few rules to determine that assertions are precise.
elements.sent-2411-823: Separating conjunctions are precise if their subcomponents are, which follows from monotonicity of the independent product (\Aref{lem:otimes-mono ).}
elements.sent-2411-824: Without this partitioning side condition, the result is not necessarily precise.
elements.sent-2411-825: Weak separating conjunctions $\osep$ and nondeterministic outcome conjunctions $\bignd$ are not precise, as there are generally many different minimal probability spaces that satisfy them, assigning different probabilities to each event.
elements.sent-2411-826: However, those assertions do obey convexity, a weaker condition, which intuitively means that $\varphi \oplus_p\varphi \Rightarrow \varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-827: We give the formal definition below.
elements.sent-2411-828: Clearly any precise assertion is convex, since the set of measures $S$ is just a singleton in that case.
elements.sent-2411-829: So, in addition to analogues of the precision rules above, we also have the following: \begin{mathpar \inferrule{ \convex{\varphi,\psi { \convex{\varphi\osep\psi \inferrule{ \convex{\varphi,\psi \substack{\varphi\Rightarrow\sure{e\mapsto 1 \psi\Rightarrow \sure{e\mapsto 0 { \convex{\varphi \oplus_{\ge p}\psi \inferrule{ \convex{\varphi \varphi\Rightarrow\sure{e\mapsto X { \convex{\textstyle\bignd_{X\in E}\varphi \end{mathpar Precision and convexity are useful for formulating entailment laws, which we provide in \Cref{fig:entailment .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-830: The first row consists of rules for the interaction between separating conjunctions and pure assertions.
elements.sent-2411-831: Weakening can be performed underneath both pure assertions and separating conjunctions.
elements.sent-2411-832: In addition, strong separation implies weak separation, and weak and strong separation are equivalent when one of the conjuncts is a pure assertion (since independence is trivial in that case).
elements.sent-2411-833: In the second row, we give some rules pertaining to $\bignd$.
elements.sent-2411-834: An outcome conjunction $\bigoplus_{X\sim d(E)}$ can be weakened to a $\bignd$ over $\supp(d(E))$, where $\supp(\bern{E ) \triangleq \{0, 1\}$ and $\supp(\unif{E ) \triangleq E$.}}
elements.sent-2411-835: A $\bignd_{X\in \{E\}}\varphi$ over a singleton set is the same as substituting $E$ for $X$ in $\varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-836: Finally, the bounds of a $\bignd$ can always be expanded, similar to how $P \Rightarrow P\vee Q$ in classical logic.
elements.sent-2411-837: The final two rows pertain to outcome conjunctions, and each rule has a corresponding one (not shown) with $\bignd$ instead of $\bigoplus$.
elements.sent-2411-838: Weakening can be performed inside of an outcome conjunction, and bound variables can be $\alpha$-renamed as long as the new variable name is fresh.
elements.sent-2411-839: As in Bluebell, the (strong) separating conjunction distributes over the outcome conjunction, so that assertions can be moved inside of an outcome conjunction, but this rule is invalid if $\sep$ is replaced by $\osep$, due to the possibility of correlations between $\psi$ and $d(E)$.
elements.sent-2411-840: Factoring assertions out of an outcome conjunction is only supported in Bluebell for almost-sure assertions $\sure P$, whereas in \pcol, it can be performed for any precise assertion, due to our semantics based on a direct sum.
elements.sent-2411-841: In fact, this exact scenario also arose in the RCond and RCase rules of \psl, where an analogous concept called supported was used to ensure soundness \cite{psl .}
elements.sent-2411-842: Replacing strong separation with weak separation, we only need $\psi$ to be convex---not precise---to factor it out of the outcome conjunction, since independence is not implied.
elements.sent-2411-843: Finally, outcome conjunctions over convex assertions that do not depend on the bound variable $X$ can be collapsed.

elements.sent-2411-861: For example, in the following program (which we previously saw in \Cref{sec:ov-precise ), the scheduler could choose to schedule the $y \coloneqq 1$ action after the sampling operation is resolved, meaning that it could make $x=y$ with probability 1, a clear correlation. \begin{equation}x \samp \bern{\tfrac12} \fatsemi y \coloneqq 0 \quad\mathlarger{\mathlarger\parallel}\quad y \coloneqq 1\end{equation} As such, the outcomes of the two threads will not be independent after being run concurrently, but rather only observably independent in some restricted event space.}

elements.sent-2411-876: The first four splitting rules enable pointwise reasoning over outcome conjunctions, similar to those of Demonic Outcome Logic \cite{zilberstein2025demonic .}

elements.sent-2411-878: The first rule, \ruleref{Split1 , requires that $\psi$ dictates the partition of the probability spaces in order to construct a final direct sum.}

elements.sent-2411-894: The final proof rule is for analyzing while loops, and proving that they almost surely terminate---that is, they terminate with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-895: The \ruleref{BoundedRank rule, shown in \Cref{fig:while-rule , is based on rules for sequential programs due to \citet{mciver2005abstraction and \citet{zilberstein2025demonic .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-896: It revolves around a loop invariant $\varphi$ and a rank.
elements.sent-2411-897: The rank $R$ must be integer-valued and bounded between $\ell$ and $h$, \ie $\varphi\Rightarrow\sure{\ell\le R\le h}$.
elements.sent-2411-898: As long as $R > \ell$, the loop continues to iterate ($\varphi\sep\sure{R>\ell}\Rightarrow\sure{b\mapsto\tru}$) and once $R$ reaches $\ell$, the loop must terminate ($\varphi\sep\sure{R=\ell \Rightarrow\sure{b\mapsto\fls}$).}
elements.sent-2411-899: The premise of \ruleref{BoundedRank guarantees that the rank strictly decreases each iteration with probability at least $p > 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-900: Since the rank is bounded between $\ell$ and $h$, this means that from any start state, the loop is guaranteed to terminate with probability at least $p^{h-\ell > 0$, which allows us to conclude that the program must almost surely terminate by the Zero-One law of \citet[Lemma 2.6.1]{mciver2005abstraction .}}
elements.sent-2411-901: Finally, since the terminating outcome $\varphi[\ell/R]$ is precise, there must be a unique minimal probability space $\Q$ that satisfies it.
elements.sent-2411-902: Similar to the soundness proof of the \ruleref{Par rule, we complete the soundness proof of the \ruleref{BoundedRank rule by showing that for all $B \in \F_\Q$ and all $\nu$ resulting from finite approximations of the loop, $\nu(B)$ converges to $\mu_\Q(B)$.}}
elements.figure-2411-016: \begin{mathpar} \ruledef{BoundedRank}{ \substack{\varphi \Rightarrow \sure{\ell \le R \le h} \varphi\sep\sure{R=\ell}\Rightarrow\sure{b\mapsto\fls} \varphi\sep\sure{R>\ell}\Rightarrow\sure{b\mapsto\tru}} \quad I\vdash_m\triple{\varphi \sep \sure{R = N > \ell}}{C}{\textstyle (\bignd_{R = \ell}^{N-1}\varphi) \oplus_{\ge p} (\bignd_{R = N}^h \varphi)} \quad \substack{ 0 < p \le 1 N \notin\free(\varphi) \precise{\varphi[\ell/R]} } }{ I\vdash_m\triple{\textstyle\bignd_{R=\ell}^h\varphi}{\whl bC}{\varphi[\ell/R]} } \end{mathpar} \caption{The {BoundedRank} rule for almost sure termination. } \label{fig:while-rule}
elements.sent-2411-903: As an example of how to apply this rule, consider the example program below.
elements.sent-2411-904: The program implements a sort of random walk where $x$ moves towards the origin with probability $\frac12$, otherwise it is updated to $y$, which may be altered by a parallel thread. \begin{equation}\code{RandWalk} \quad=\quad \whl{x > 0}{\;\; \underbrace{ b \samp \textstyle\bern{\frac12}\fatsemi \iftf{b}{ x \coloneqq x-1 }{ x \coloneqq y }}_{C_body}}\end{equation} As long as the value of $y$ is bounded, this loop almost surely terminates, which we can prove using \ruleref{BoundedRank , subject to the resource invariant $I = y \in \{0, \ldots, 5\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-905: We will sketch the proof of almost sure termination here, the full derivation is shown in \Aref{app:ast-example .}
elements.sent-2411-906: The loop invariant is $\varphi\triangleq{\sure{x \mapsto R \sep 0\le R\le 5 \sep \own(b) }$, essentially just stating that $x$ is between 0 and 5.}
elements.sent-2411-907: The rank $R$ is the value of $x$, and so $R$ decreases each iteration with probability at least $\frac12$.
elements.sent-2411-908: However, $x$ may also be updated to $y$, which is nondeterministic, requiring a use of the \ruleref{Exists rule to conclude that $\bignd_{R=1 ^5\sure{x \mapsto R}$ after the command $x \coloneqq y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-909: Clearly $\bignd_{R=1 ^5\sure{x \mapsto R}$ is not precise, so the resulting triple is not strongly frame preserving.}
elements.sent-2411-910: Ultimately, we get the following weak triple for the loop body, which states that the rank strictly decreases with probability at least $\frac12$. \begin{equation}\label{eq:ast-body} y\in\{0, \ldots, 5\} \vdash_\wk\triple{\sure{x \mapsto N}}{ C_\mathsf{body}}{\textstyle \big( \bignd_{R=0}^{N-1}\sure{x \mapsto R} \big) \oplus_{\ge\frac12} \big( \bignd_{R=N}^{5}\sure{x \mapsto R} \big) }\end{equation} At this stage, the weak triple signifies that we do not know exactly how likely the program is to terminate; we can only bound the probability.
elements.sent-2411-911: Indeed, the scheduler can influence the likelihood that $x$ takes on particular values, and can force $x$ to be correlated with other state.
elements.sent-2411-912: For example, suppose we wanted to apply the \ruleref{Frame rule with $z \sim \unif{\{0,\ldots, 5\}}$.}
elements.sent-2411-913: The scheduler could choose to always make $y$ and $z$ equal, in which case $z \sim \unif{\{0,\ldots, 5\}}$ would not be independent of the postcondition of (\ref{eq:ast-body ).}
elements.sent-2411-914: However, the point of \ruleref{BoundedRank is that the scheduler cannot affect the probability of eventual termination.}
elements.sent-2411-915: As such, the postcondition at the end of the execution is $\varphi[0/R]$, which is equivalent to the precise assertion $\sure{x\mapsto 0}$, allowing us to apply \ruleref{Strengthen to get the following strong triple for the entire program. \begin{equation}y \in \{0, \ldots, 5\} \vdash \triple{\sure{x \in \{0, \ldots, 5\}}}{\code{RandWalk}}{\sure{x\mapsto 0}}\end{equation} One remarkable aspect of \pcol is that the triple above also implies that $\code{RandWalk}$ almost surely terminates when run in parallel with any other almost surely terminating program that obeys the resource invariant $I$.}

elements.sent-2411-994: In \pcol, we combine the two steps, avoiding the need to write down a specification program by building quantitative reasoning tools into the logic itself.

elements.sent-2411-1037: In this section, we give details on the Pomsets with Formulae semantic model due to \citet{zilberstein2025denotational .}
elements.sent-2411-1038: Whereas standard pomsets (partially ordered multisets) use a partial order to record the causality between atomic actions in the program (elements of a multiset) \cite{gischer1988equational,pratt1986modeling , pomsets with formulae add a Boolean formula to each node, which records which tests must succeed or fail to reach that point in the execution.}
elements.sent-2411-1039: This is necessary to capture probabilistic concurrency, since tests may not always pass or fail, but rather they may only have some probability of passing, and so both paths must be represented in a single semantic structure.
elements.sent-2411-1040: We briefly introduce pomsets with formulae here, refer to \citet{zilberstein2025denotational for a more complete treatment.}
elements.sent-2411-1041: Let $\lab \triangleq \act \cup \test \cup \{ \bullet, \bot \}$, which can be either an action or test (from \Cref{fig:syntax ), a no-op ($\bullet$), or undetermined ($\bot$).}
elements.sent-2411-1042: Although $\bot$ labels will never appear in the denotation of a complete program, they are used to indicate that a finite structure is an approximation of an infinite one, \ie in the semantics of while loops.
elements.sent-2411-1043: Also, let $\Nodes$ be a countable universe of identifiers and $\Form$ be the set of Boolean formulae over $\Nodes$.
elements.sent-2411-1044: For some $\psi \in \Form$, we write $\sat(\psi)$ to indicate that $\psi$ is satisfiable, and $\free(\psi) \subseteq \Nodes$ is the set of variables referenced in $\psi$.
elements.sent-2411-1045: We now define the underlying structure.
elements.sent-2411-1046: For some $\alpha \in \lpo$, we will often use $N_\alpha$, $<_\alpha$, $\lambda_\alpha$, and $\varphi_\alpha$ to refer to its parts.
elements.sent-2411-1047: The first three components are standard in pomset semantics.
elements.sent-2411-1048: The order denotes causality, so $x < y$ means that the action $\lambda(x)$ must be scheduled before $\lambda(y)$.
elements.sent-2411-1049: The order is partial (not total) because actions that occur in parallel are not related.
elements.sent-2411-1050: Formulae are a new addition to this structure, allowing us to encode guarded branching in the structure too.
elements.sent-2411-1051: For any node $x\in N$, $\varphi(x)$ is a satisfiable formula comprised of nodes appearing earlier in the trace, since the corresponding tests must be resolved before executing later actions.
elements.sent-2411-1052: In addition, formulae can only become stronger as the trace goes on, since dependencies on more and more tests are accumulated over time.
elements.sent-2411-1053: Two LPOFs are isomorphic, denoted $\alpha \equiv \beta$ iff there is a bijection $f \colon N_\alpha \to N_\beta$ such that $x <_\alpha y$ iff $f(x) <_\beta f(y)$, $\lambda_\alpha = \lambda_\beta \circ f$, and $\varphi_\alpha = f^{-1 \circ \varphi_\beta \circ f$, where $f^{-1 (\psi)$ renames the variables of $\psi$ in the obvious way.}}
elements.sent-2411-1054: Given that, a pomset with formulae is an equivalence class of LPOFs.
elements.sent-2411-1055: A pomset with formulae (or, simply, a pomset) $\Alpha \in \pom$ is an isomorphism class of LPOFs. \begin{equation}\pom \triangleq \{ [\alpha] \mid \alpha \in \lpo \}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1056: Pomsets with formulae have many nice domain-theoretic properties.
elements.sent-2411-1057: For example, they have a DCPO (directed complete partial order) structure, meaning that Scott continuous operations over them have least fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-1058: Roughly speaking $\Alpha \lepom \Beta$ iff $\Beta$ is obtained by replacing nodes labelled $\bot$ in $\Alpha$ with a new, larger structure.
elements.sent-2411-1059: We will see a concrete example of this shortly when we discuss the semantics of loops.
elements.sent-2411-1060: Infinite pomsets with formulae can also be represented as the supremum of their finite approximation, and monotone operations on those finite approximations can be extended to continuous operations on infinite pomsets.
elements.sent-2411-1061: This is useful, since it is not possible to define operations inductively on infinite structures, as we will see in \Cref{sec:linearization .}
elements.figure-2411-019: \begin{equation}\de{\skp} \triangleq \singleton\bullet \de{ {C_1 \fatsemi C_2} } \triangleq \de{C_1} \fatsemi \de{C_2} \de{C_1 \parallel C_2} \triangleq \de{C_1} \parallel \de{C_2} \de{\iftf b{C_1}{C_2}} \triangleq guard(b, \de{C_1}, \de{C_2}) \de{\whl bC} \triangleq lfp\left(\Phi_{\tuple{b, C}}\right) \quadwhere\ \Phi_{\tuple{b, C}}(\Alpha) \triangleq \guard\left(b, \de{C}\fatsemi \Alpha, \singleton{\bullet}\right) \de{a} \triangleq \singleton{a}\end{equation} \caption{Trace semantics for commands $\de{-} \colon \mathsf{Cmd} \to \pom$.} \label{fig:trace-semantics}
elements.sent-2411-1062: The trace semantics $\de{- \colon \mathsf{Cmd \to \pom$, shown in \Cref{fig:trace-semantics , interprets every command as a pomset with formulae.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1063: We use several operators to construct pomsets.
elements.sent-2411-1064: First, $\singleton- \colon \lab \to \pom$ constructs a singleton pomset with the given label.
elements.sent-2411-1065: This is used for the semantics of $\skp$ and actions $a \in \act$.
elements.sent-2411-1066: Next, we have three combinators for combining pomsets, which we demonstrate pictorially below.
elements.sent-2411-1067: The first is sequential composition $\Alpha\fatsemi\Beta$, which constructs a pomset where all the actions from $\Alpha$ occur before those in $\Beta$.
elements.sent-2411-1068: In the diagram, $a_1\to a_2$ indicates causality ($a_1$ occurs before $a_2)$ The second combinator is guarded choice $\guard(b, \Alpha, \Beta)$, where the test $b$ becomes the new root and all formulae of the nodes in $\Alpha$ and $\Beta$ are updated to indicate that the new root must pass or fail, respectively, as indicated by the arrows labelled $\mathsf{T}$ and $\mathsf{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-1069: Finally, parallel composition $\Alpha \parallel \Beta$ joins the two pomsets with a new no-op root, so that the actions of $\Alpha$ and $\Beta$ can be interleaved without restriction.
elements.sent-2411-1070: This means that the least fixed point is equal to the supremum of the finite unrollings of $\Phi_{\tuple{b, C }$, as shown in the following diagram. \begin{equation}\arraycolsep=.25em {cccccccc} \bot \lepom \vcenter{\vbox{\xymatrix@R=3pt@C=4pt{ \bot a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows }}} \lepom \vcenter{\vbox{\xymatrix@R=3pt@C=4pt{ \bot a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows }}} \lepom\cdots\lepom \vcenter{\vbox{\xymatrix@R=3pt@C=4pt{ \vdots a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows }}} \Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^0(\singleton\bot) \Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^1(\singleton\bot) \Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^2(\singleton\bot) \sup_{n\in\mathbb N}\Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^n(\singleton\bot) \end{equation} In each unrolling, $\bot$ appears further and further from the root until, in the supremum, it is pushed infinitely far away.}
elements.formula-2411-162: \begin{equation}\arraycolsep=.25em {cccccccc} \bot \lepom \vcenter{\vbox{\xymatrix@R=3pt@C=4pt{ \bot a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows }}} \lepom \vcenter{\vbox{\xymatrix@R=3pt@C=4pt{ \bot a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows }}} \lepom\cdots\lepom \vcenter{\vbox{\xymatrix@R=3pt@C=4pt{ \vdots a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows a \ar[u] \fork b \guardarrows }}} \Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^0(\singleton\bot) \Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^1(\singleton\bot) \Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^2(\singleton\bot) \sup_{n\in\mathbb N}\Phi_{\tuple{a, b}}^n(\singleton\bot) \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-1072: In the context of probabilistic programs, it is important to include the infinite spine, as the probability of continuing to execute may only converge to 0 in the limit.
elements.sent-2411-1073: The benefit of the pomset model is that it is fully compositional; the parallel composition of two commands is interpreted as a straightforward combination of their syntactic structures.
elements.sent-2411-1074: However, for the purposes of designing a program logic, we need a state transformer model, which maps each input state to a convex set of distributions over output states.
elements.sent-2411-1075: For this purpose, \citet{zilberstein2025denotational also define linearization $\lin \colon \pom \to \mem{S \to \C(\mem{S )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1077: Linearizing a node $\linnode$ does case analysis on the label of the node to decide how to proceed.
elements.sent-2411-1078: If the node is an action, then the action is evaluated and composed with the linearization of the remainder of the LPOF.
elements.sent-2411-1079: If the node is a test, then the result of the test is added to the path condition.
elements.sent-2411-1080: If the node is $\bot$, then the execution is halted.
elements.sent-2411-1081: If it is a no-op, then the execution simply continues.
elements.sent-2411-1082: To linearize an entire finite LPOF, we simply perform a nondeterministic choice over all the next nodes, linearize those nodes, and then proceed processing the rest of the structure recursively.
elements.sent-2411-1083: To make the recursion well-founded, the structure must be finite, but we extend to the infinite case later.
elements.sent-2411-1084: To linearize a finite pomset $\Alpha$, we simply linearize any representative LPOF $\alpha \in \Alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-1085: Finally, to linearize an infinite pomset, we take the supremum over the linearization of all the finite approximations, where $\Alpha' \ll \Alpha$ indicates that $\Alpha'$ is a finite approximation of $\Alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-1086: Since most operations are continuous, we get, \eg that the supremum of finite approximations of a sequential composition is equal to the sequential composition over the supremum of finite approximations: \begin{equation}\sup_{\Alpha'\ll\Alpha}\sup_{\Beta'\ll\Beta} \Alpha' \fatsemi \Beta' = \left(\sup_{\Alpha'\ll\Alpha} \Alpha'\right) \fatsemi \left( \sup_{\Beta'\ll\Beta} \Beta'\right) = \Alpha \fatsemi \Beta\end{equation} The exception to this is for parallel composition, which is not continuous for technical reasons.
elements.sent-2411-1087: However, if we define $\ll_1$ as follows: \begin{equation}\GGamma \ll_1 \GGamma' \qquad iff \qquad \GGamma \ll \GGamma' \quadand\quad root(\GGamma) = root(\GGamma') = \fork \quador\quad root(\GGamma') \neq \fork\end{equation} where $\mathsf{root (\GGamma)$ is the label of the root node of the pomset $\GGamma$, then we get the following weaker pseudo-continuity property: \begin{equation}\sup_{\Alpha'\ll_1\Alpha}\sup_{\Beta'\ll_1\Beta} \Alpha' \parallel \Beta' = \left(\sup_{\Alpha'\ll_1\Alpha} \Alpha'\right) \parallel \left( \sup_{\Beta'\ll_1\Beta} \Beta'\right) = \Alpha \parallel \Beta\end{equation}.}
